title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: ease be done by consigning to one in these ports?” I answered that
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: certainly it would be most easy, but that we solicitors had a system of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: agency one for the other, so that local work could be done locally on
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: instruction from any solicitor, so that the client, simply placing
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: himself in the hands of one man, could have his wishes carried out by
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: him without further trouble.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “But,” said he, “I could be at liberty to direct myself. Is it not so?”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: available, he suddenly stood up and said:--

author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: thought.

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

text: I could.

author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula

author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

text: and mystery which seemed closing around me.

author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula

author: Stoker, Bram

text: of the courtyard. Looking out on this, I felt that I was indeed in
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: prison, and I seemed to want a breath of fresh air, though it were of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the night. I am beginning to feel this nocturnal existence tell on me.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: It is destroying my nerve. I start at my own shadow, and am full of all

title: Dracula

author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

text: and amuse a man when he is a prisoner. But my very feelings changed to

author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

text: be no delusion. I saw the fingers and toes grasp the corners of the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

text: using every projection and inequality move downwards with considerable

author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

text: and envelope in the flame of the lamp till they were consumed. Then he
title: Dracula

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “The letter to Hawkins--that I shall, of course, send on, since it is
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: yours. Your letters are sacred to me. Your pardon, my friend, that
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: envelope. I could only redirect it and hand it to him in silence. When
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: he went out of the room I could hear the key turn softly. A minute later
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I went over and tried it, and the door was locked.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: When, an hour or two after, the Count came quietly into the room, his
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: coming awakened me, for I had gone to sleep on the sofa. He was very
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: courteous and very cheery in his manner, and seeing that I had been
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sleeping, he said:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “So, my friend, you are tired? Get to bed. There is the surest rest. I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: may not have the pleasure to talk to-night, since there are many labours
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to me; but you will sleep, I pray.” I passed to my room and went to bed,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and, strange to say, slept without dreaming. Despair has its own calms.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _31 May._--This morning when I woke I thought I would provide myself
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: with some paper and envelopes from my bag and keep them in my pocket, so
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: that I might write in case I should get an opportunity, but again a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: surprise, again a shock!
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Every scrap of paper was gone, and with it all my notes, my memoranda,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: relating to railways and travel, my letter of credit, in fact all that
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: might be useful to me were I once outside the castle. I sat and pondered
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: awhile, and then some thought occurred to me, and I made search of my
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: portmanteau and in the wardrobe where I had placed my clothes.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: The suit in which I had travelled was gone, and also my overcoat and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: rug; I could find no trace of them anywhere. This looked like some new
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: scheme of villainy....
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _17 June._--This morning, as I was sitting on the edge of my bed
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: cudgelling my brains, I heard without a cracking of whips and pounding
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and scraping of horses’ feet up the rocky path beyond the courtyard.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: With joy I hurried to the window, and saw drive into the yard two great
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: leiter-wagons, each drawn by eight sturdy horses, and at the head of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: each pair a Slovak, with his wide hat, great nail-studded belt, dirty
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sheepskin, and high boots. They had also their long staves in hand. I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: ran to the door, intending to descend and try and join them through the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: main hall, as I thought that way might be opened for them. Again a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: shock: my door was fastened on the outside.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Then I ran to the window and cried to them. They looked up at me
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: stupidly and pointed, but just then the “hetman” of the Szgany came out,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and seeing them pointing to my window, said something, at which they
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: laughed. Henceforth no effort of mine, no piteous cry or agonised
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: entreaty, would make them even look at me. They resolutely turned away.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: The leiter-wagons contained great, square boxes, with handles of thick
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: rope; these were evidently empty by the ease with which the Slovaks
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: handled them, and by their resonance as they were roughly moved. When
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: they were all unloaded and packed in a great heap in one corner of the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: yard, the Slovaks were given some money by the Szgany, and spitting on
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: it for luck, lazily went each to his horse’s head. Shortly afterwards, I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: heard the cracking of their whips die away in the distance.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _24 June, before morning._--Last night the Count left me early, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: locked himself into his own room. As soon as I dared I ran up the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: winding stair, and looked out of the window, which opened south. I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: thought I would watch for the Count, for there is something going on.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: The Szgany are quartered somewhere in the castle and are doing work of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: some kind. I know it, for now and then I hear a far-away muffled sound
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: as of mattock and spade, and, whatever it is, it must be the end of some
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: ruthless villainy.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I had been at the window somewhat less than half an hour, when I saw
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: something coming out of the Count’s window. I drew back and watched
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: carefully, and saw the whole man emerge. It was a new shock to me to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: find that he had on the suit of clothes which I had worn whilst
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: travelling here, and slung over his shoulder the terrible bag which I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: had seen the women take away. There could be no doubt as to his quest,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and in my garb, too! This, then, is his new scheme of evil: that he will
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: allow others to see me, as they think, so that he may both leave
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: evidence that I have been seen in the towns or villages posting my own
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: letters, and that any wickedness which he may do shall by the local
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: people be attributed to me.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: It makes me rage to think that this can go on, and whilst I am shut up
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: here, a veritable prisoner, but without that protection of the law which
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: is even a criminal’s right and consolation.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I thought I would watch for the Count’s return, and for a long time sat
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: doggedly at the window. Then I began to notice that there were some
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: quaint little specks floating in the rays of the moonlight. They were
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: like the tiniest grains of dust, and they whirled round and gathered in
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: clusters in a nebulous sort of way. I watched them with a sense of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: soothing, and a sort of calm stole over me. I leaned back in the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: embrasure in a more comfortable position, so that I could enjoy more
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: fully the aërial gambolling.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Something made me start up, a low, piteous howling of dogs somewhere far
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: below in the valley, which was hidden from my sight. Louder it seemed to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: ring in my ears, and the floating motes of dust to take new shapes to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the sound as they danced in the moonlight. I felt myself struggling to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: awake to some call of my instincts; nay, my very soul was struggling,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and my half-remembered sensibilities were striving to answer the call. I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: was becoming hypnotised! Quicker and quicker danced the dust; the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: moonbeams seemed to quiver as they went by me into the mass of gloom
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: beyond. More and more they gathered till they seemed to take dim phantom
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: shapes. And then I started, broad awake and in full possession of my
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: senses, and ran screaming from the place. The phantom shapes, which were
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: becoming gradually materialised from the moonbeams, were those of the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: three ghostly women to whom I was doomed. I fled, and felt somewhat
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: safer in my own room, where there was no moonlight and where the lamp
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: was burning brightly.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: When a couple of hours had passed I heard something stirring in the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Count’s room, something like a sharp wail quickly suppressed; and then
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: there was silence, deep, awful silence, which chilled me. With a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: beating heart, I tried the door; but I was locked in my prison, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: could do nothing. I sat down and simply cried.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: As I sat I heard a sound in the courtyard without--the agonised cry of a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: woman. I rushed to the window, and throwing it up, peered out between
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the bars. There, indeed, was a woman with dishevelled hair, holding her
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: hands over her heart as one distressed with running. She was leaning
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: against a corner of the gateway. When she saw my face at the window she
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: threw herself forward, and shouted in a voice laden with menace:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Monster, give me my child!”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: She threw herself on her knees, and raising up her hands, cried the same
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: words in tones which wrung my heart. Then she tore her hair and beat her
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: breast, and abandoned herself to all the violences of extravagant
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: emotion. Finally, she threw herself forward, and, though I could not see
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: her, I could hear the beating of her naked hands against the door.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Somewhere high overhead, probably on the tower, I heard the voice of the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Count calling in his harsh, metallic whisper. His call seemed to be
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: answered from far and wide by the howling of wolves. Before many minutes
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: had passed a pack of them poured, like a pent-up dam when liberated,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: through the wide entrance into the courtyard.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: There was no cry from the woman, and the howling of the wolves was but
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: short. Before long they streamed away singly, licking their lips.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I could not pity her, for I knew now what had become of her child, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: she was better dead.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: What shall I do? what can I do? How can I escape from this dreadful
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: thing of night and gloom and fear?
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _25 June, morning._--No man knows till he has suffered from the night
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: how sweet and how dear to his heart and eye the morning can be. When the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sun grew so high this morning that it struck the top of the great
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: gateway opposite my window, the high spot which it touched seemed to me
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: as if the dove from the ark had lighted there. My fear fell from me as
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: if it had been a vaporous garment which dissolved in the warmth. I must
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: take action of some sort whilst the courage of the day is upon me. Last
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: night one of my post-dated letters went to post, the first of that fatal
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: series which is to blot out the very traces of my existence from the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: earth.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Let me not think of it. Action!
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: It has always been at night-time that I have been molested or
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: threatened, or in some way in danger or in fear. I have not yet seen the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Count in the daylight. Can it be that he sleeps when others wake, that
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: he may be awake whilst they sleep? If I could only get into his room!
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: But there is no possible way. The door is always locked, no way for me.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Yes, there is a way, if one dares to take it. Where his body has gone
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: why may not another body go? I have seen him myself crawl from his
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: window. Why should not I imitate him, and go in by his window? The
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: chances are desperate, but my need is more desperate still. I shall risk
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: it. At the worst it can only be death; and a man’s death is not a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: calf’s, and the dreaded Hereafter may still be open to me. God help me
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: in my task! Good-bye, Mina, if I fail; good-bye, my faithful friend and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: second father; good-bye, all, and last of all Mina!
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _Same day, later._--I have made the effort, and God, helping me, have
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: come safely back to this room. I must put down every detail in order. I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: went whilst my courage was fresh straight to the window on the south
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: side, and at once got outside on the narrow ledge of stone which runs
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: around the building on this side. The stones are big and roughly cut,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and the mortar has by process of time been washed away between them. I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: took off my boots, and ventured out on the desperate way. I looked down
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: once, so as to make sure that a sudden glimpse of the awful depth would
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: not overcome me, but after that kept my eyes away from it. I knew pretty
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: well the direction and distance of the Count’s window, and made for it
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: as well as I could, having regard to the opportunities available. I did
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: not feel dizzy--I suppose I was too excited--and the time seemed
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: ridiculously short till I found myself standing on the window-sill and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: trying to raise up the sash. I was filled with agitation, however, when
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I bent down and slid feet foremost in through the window. Then I looked
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: around for the Count, but, with surprise and gladness, made a discovery.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: The room was empty! It was barely furnished with odd things, which
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: seemed to have never been used; the furniture was something the same
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: style as that in the south rooms, and was covered with dust. I looked
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: for the key, but it was not in the lock, and I could not find it
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: anywhere. The only thing I found was a great heap of gold in one
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: corner--gold of all kinds, Roman, and British, and Austrian, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Hungarian, and Greek and Turkish money, covered with a film of dust, as
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: though it had lain long in the ground. None of it that I noticed was
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: less than three hundred years old. There were also chains and ornaments,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: some jewelled, but all of them old and stained.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: At one corner of the room was a heavy door. I tried it, for, since I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: could not find the key of the room or the key of the outer door, which
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: was the main object of my search, I must make further examination, or
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: all my efforts would be in vain. It was open, and led through a stone
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: passage to a circular stairway, which went steeply down. I descended,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: minding carefully where I went, for the stairs were dark, being only lit
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: by loopholes in the heavy masonry. At the bottom there was a dark,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: tunnel-like passage, through which came a deathly, sickly odour, the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: odour of old earth newly turned. As I went through the passage the smell
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: grew closer and heavier. At last I pulled open a heavy door which stood
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: ajar, and found myself in an old, ruined chapel, which had evidently
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: been used as a graveyard. The roof was broken, and in two places were
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: steps leading to vaults, but the ground had recently been dug over, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the earth placed in great wooden boxes, manifestly those which had been
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: brought by the Slovaks. There was nobody about, and I made search for
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: any further outlet, but there was none. Then I went over every inch of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the ground, so as not to lose a chance. I went down even into the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: vaults, where the dim light struggled, although to do so was a dread to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: my very soul. Into two of these I went, but saw nothing except fragments
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: of old coffins and piles of dust; in the third, however, I made a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: discovery.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: There, in one of the great boxes, of which there were fifty in all, on a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: pile of newly dug earth, lay the Count! He was either dead or asleep, I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: could not say which--for the eyes were open and stony, but without the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: glassiness of death--and the cheeks had the warmth of life through all
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: their pallor; the lips were as red as ever. But there was no sign of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: movement, no pulse, no breath, no beating of the heart. I bent over him,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and tried to find any sign of life, but in vain. He could not have lain
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: there long, for the earthy smell would have passed away in a few hours.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: By the side of the box was its cover, pierced with holes here and there.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I thought he might have the keys on him, but when I went to search I saw
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the dead eyes, and in them, dead though they were, such a look of hate,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: though unconscious of me or my presence, that I fled from the place, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: leaving the Count’s room by the window, crawled again up the castle
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: wall. Regaining my room, I threw myself panting upon the bed and tried
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to think....
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _29 June._--To-day is the date of my last letter, and the Count has
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: taken steps to prove that it was genuine, for again I saw him leave the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: castle by the same window, and in my clothes. As he went down the wall,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: lizard fashion, I wished I had a gun or some lethal weapon, that I might
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: destroy him; but I fear that no weapon wrought alone by man’s hand would
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: have any effect on him. I dared not wait to see him return, for I feared
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to see those weird sisters. I came back to the library, and read there
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: till I fell asleep.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I was awakened by the Count, who looked at me as grimly as a man can
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: look as he said:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “To-morrow, my friend, we must part. You return to your beautiful
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: England, I to some work which may have such an end that we may never
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: meet. Your letter home has been despatched; to-morrow I shall not be
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: here, but all shall be ready for your journey. In the morning come the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Szgany, who have some labours of their own here, and also come some
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Slovaks. When they have gone, my carriage shall come for you, and shall
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: bear you to the Borgo Pass to meet the diligence from Bukovina to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Bistritz. But I am in hopes that I shall see more of you at Castle
title: Dracula

text: Whitby, for it lies right over the town, and has a full view of the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: harbour and all up the bay to where the headland called Kettleness
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: stretches out into the sea. It descends so steeply over the harbour that
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: part of the bank has fallen away, and some of the graves have been
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: destroyed. In one place part of the stonework of the graves stretches
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: out over the sandy pathway far below. There are walks, with seats beside
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: them, through the churchyard; and people go and sit there all day long
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: looking at the beautiful view and enjoying the breeze. I shall come and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sit here very often myself and work. Indeed, I am writing now, with my
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: book on my knee, and listening to the talk of three old men who are
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sitting beside me. They seem to do nothing all day but sit up here and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: talk.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: The harbour lies below me, with, on the far side, one long granite wall
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: stretching out into the sea, with a curve outwards at the end of it, in
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the middle of which is a lighthouse. A heavy sea-wall runs along outside
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: of it. On the near side, the sea-wall makes an elbow crooked inversely,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and its end too has a lighthouse. Between the two piers there is a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: narrow opening into the harbour, which then suddenly widens.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: It is nice at high water; but when the tide is out it shoals away to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: nothing, and there is merely the stream of the Esk, running between
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: banks of sand, with rocks here and there. Outside the harbour on this
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: side there rises for about half a mile a great reef, the sharp edge of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: which runs straight out from behind the south lighthouse. At the end of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: it is a buoy with a bell, which swings in bad weather, and sends in a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: mournful sound on the wind. They have a legend here that when a ship is
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: lost bells are heard out at sea. I must ask the old man about this; he
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: is coming this way....
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: He is a funny old man. He must be awfully old, for his face is all
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: gnarled and twisted like the bark of a tree. He tells me that he is
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: nearly a hundred, and that he was a sailor in the Greenland fishing
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: fleet when Waterloo was fought. He is, I am afraid, a very sceptical
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: person, for when I asked him about the bells at sea and the White Lady
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: at the abbey he said very brusquely:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “I wouldn’t fash masel’ about them, miss. Them things be all wore out.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Mind, I don’t say that they never was, but I do say that they wasn’t in
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: my time. They be all very well for comers and trippers, an’ the like,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: but not for a nice young lady like you. Them feet-folks from York and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Leeds that be always eatin’ cured herrin’s an’ drinkin’ tea an’ lookin’
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: out to buy cheap jet would creed aught. I wonder masel’ who’d be
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: bothered tellin’ lies to them--even the newspapers, which is full of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: fool-talk.” I thought he would be a good person to learn interesting
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: things from, so I asked him if he would mind telling me something about
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the whale-fishing in the old days. He was just settling himself to begin
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: when the clock struck six, whereupon he laboured to get up, and said:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “I must gang ageeanwards home now, miss. My grand-daughter doesn’t like
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to be kept waitin’ when the tea is ready, for it takes me time to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: crammle aboon the grees, for there be a many of ’em; an’, miss, I lack
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: belly-timber sairly by the clock.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: He hobbled away, and I could see him hurrying, as well as he could, down
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the steps. The steps are a great feature on the place. They lead from
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the town up to the church, there are hundreds of them--I do not know how
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: many--and they wind up in a delicate curve; the slope is so gentle that
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: a horse could easily walk up and down them. I think they must originally
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: have had something to do with the abbey. I shall go home too. Lucy went
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: out visiting with her mother, and as they were only duty calls, I did
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: not go. They will be home by this.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _1 August._--I came up here an hour ago with Lucy, and we had a most
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: interesting talk with my old friend and the two others who always come
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and join him. He is evidently the Sir Oracle of them, and I should think
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: must have been in his time a most dictatorial person. He will not admit
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: anything, and downfaces everybody. If he can’t out-argue them he bullies
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: them, and then takes their silence for agreement with his views. Lucy
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: was looking sweetly pretty in her white lawn frock; she has got a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: beautiful colour since she has been here. I noticed that the old men did
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: not lose any time in coming up and sitting near her when we sat down.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: She is so sweet with old people; I think they all fell in love with her
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: on the spot. Even my old man succumbed and did not contradict her, but
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: gave me double share instead. I got him on the subject of the legends,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and he went off at once into a sort of sermon. I must try to remember it
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and put it down:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “It be all fool-talk, lock, stock, and barrel; that’s what it be, an’
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: nowt else. These bans an’ wafts an’ boh-ghosts an’ barguests an’ bogles
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: an’ all anent them is only fit to set bairns an’ dizzy women
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: a-belderin’. They be nowt but air-blebs. They, an’ all grims an’ signs
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: an’ warnin’s, be all invented by parsons an’ illsome beuk-bodies an’
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: railway touters to skeer an’ scunner hafflin’s, an’ to get folks to do
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: somethin’ that they don’t other incline to. It makes me ireful to think
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: o’ them. Why, it’s them that, not content with printin’ lies on paper
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: an’ preachin’ them out of pulpits, does want to be cuttin’ them on the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: tombstones. Look here all around you in what airt ye will; all them
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: steans, holdin’ up their heads as well as they can out of their pride,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: is acant--simply tumblin’ down with the weight o’ the lies wrote on
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: them, ‘Here lies the body’ or ‘Sacred to the memory’ wrote on all of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: them, an’ yet in nigh half of them there bean’t no bodies at all; an’
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the memories of them bean’t cared a pinch of snuff about, much less
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sacred. Lies all of them, nothin’ but lies of one kind or another! My
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: gog, but it’ll be a quare scowderment at the Day of Judgment when they
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: come tumblin’ up in their death-sarks, all jouped together an’ tryin’ to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: drag their tombsteans with them to prove how good they was; some of them
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: trimmlin’ and ditherin’, with their hands that dozzened an’ slippy from
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: lyin’ in the sea that they can’t even keep their grup o’ them.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I could see from the old fellow’s self-satisfied air and the way in
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: which he looked round for the approval of his cronies that he was
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “showing off,” so I put in a word to keep him going:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Oh, Mr. Swales, you can’t be serious. Surely these tombstones are not
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: all wrong?”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Yabblins! There may be a poorish few not wrong, savin’ where they make
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: out the people too good; for there be folk that do think a balm-bowl be
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: like the sea, if only it be their own. The whole thing be only lies. Now
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: look you here; you come here a stranger, an’ you see this kirk-garth.” I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: nodded, for I thought it better to assent, though I did not quite
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: understand his dialect. I knew it had something to do with the church.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: He went on: “And you consate that all these steans be aboon folk that be
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: happed here, snod an’ snog?” I assented again. “Then that be just where
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the lie comes in. Why, there be scores of these lay-beds that be toom as
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: old Dun’s ’bacca-box on Friday night.” He nudged one of his companions,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and they all laughed. “And my gog! how could they be otherwise? Look at
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: that one, the aftest abaft the bier-bank: read it!” I went over and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: read:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Edward Spencelagh, master mariner, murdered by pirates off the coast of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Andres, April, 1854, æt. 30.” When I came back Mr. Swales went on:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Who brought him home, I wonder, to hap him here? Murdered off the coast
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: of Andres! an’ you consated his body lay under! Why, I could name ye a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: dozen whose bones lie in the Greenland seas above”--he pointed
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: northwards--“or where the currents may have drifted them. There be the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: steans around ye. Ye can, with your young eyes, read the small-print of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the lies from here. This Braithwaite Lowrey--I knew his father, lost in
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the _Lively_ off Greenland in ’20; or Andrew Woodhouse, drowned in the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: same seas in 1777; or John Paxton, drowned off Cape Farewell a year
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: later; or old John Rawlings, whose grandfather sailed with me, drowned
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: in the Gulf of Finland in ’50. Do ye think that all these men will have
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to make a rush to Whitby when the trumpet sounds? I have me antherums
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: aboot it! I tell ye that when they got here they’d be jommlin’ an’
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: jostlin’ one another that way that it ’ud be like a fight up on the ice
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: in the old days, when we’d be at one another from daylight to dark, an’
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: tryin’ to tie up our cuts by the light of the aurora borealis.” This was
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: evidently local pleasantry, for the old man cackled over it, and his
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: cronies joined in with gusto.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “But,” I said, “surely you are not quite correct, for you start on the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: assumption that all the poor people, or their spirits, will have to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: take their tombstones with them on the Day of Judgment. Do you think
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: that will be really necessary?”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Well, what else be they tombstones for? Answer me that, miss!”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “To please their relatives, I suppose.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “To please their relatives, you suppose!” This he said with intense
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: scorn. “How will it pleasure their relatives to know that lies is wrote
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: over them, and that everybody in the place knows that they be lies?” He
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: pointed to a stone at our feet which had been laid down as a slab, on
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: which the seat was rested, close to the edge of the cliff. “Read the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: lies on that thruff-stean,” he said. The letters were upside down to me
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: from where I sat, but Lucy was more opposite to them, so she leant over
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and read:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Sacred to the memory of George Canon, who died, in the hope of a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: glorious resurrection, on July, 29, 1873, falling from the rocks at
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Kettleness. This tomb was erected by his sorrowing mother to her dearly
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: beloved son. ‘He was the only son of his mother, and she was a widow.’
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Really, Mr. Swales, I don’t see anything very funny in that!” She spoke
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: her comment very gravely and somewhat severely.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Ye don’t see aught funny! Ha! ha! But that’s because ye don’t gawm the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sorrowin’ mother was a hell-cat that hated him because he was
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: acrewk’d--a regular lamiter he was--an’ he hated her so that he
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: committed suicide in order that she mightn’t get an insurance she put on
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: his life. He blew nigh the top of his head off with an old musket that
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: they had for scarin’ the crows with. ’Twarn’t for crows then, for it
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: brought the clegs and the dowps to him. That’s the way he fell off the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: rocks. And, as to hopes of a glorious resurrection, I’ve often heard him
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: say masel’ that he hoped he’d go to hell, for his mother was so pious
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: that she’d be sure to go to heaven, an’ he didn’t want to addle where
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: she was. Now isn’t that stean at any rate”--he hammered it with his
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: stick as he spoke--“a pack of lies? and won’t it make Gabriel keckle
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: when Geordie comes pantin’ up the grees with the tombstean balanced on
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: his hump, and asks it to be took as evidence!”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I did not know what to say, but Lucy turned the conversation as she
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: said, rising up:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Oh, why did you tell us of this? It is my favourite seat, and I cannot
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: leave it; and now I find I must go on sitting over the grave of a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: suicide.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “That won’t harm ye, my pretty; an’ it may make poor Geordie gladsome to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: have so trim a lass sittin’ on his lap. That won’t hurt ye. Why, I’ve
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sat here off an’ on for nigh twenty years past, an’ it hasn’t done me
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: no harm. Don’t ye fash about them as lies under ye, or that doesn’ lie
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: there either! It’ll be time for ye to be getting scart when ye see the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: tombsteans all run away with, and the place as bare as a stubble-field.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: There’s the clock, an’ I must gang. My service to ye, ladies!” And off
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: he hobbled.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Lucy and I sat awhile, and it was all so beautiful before us that we
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: took hands as we sat; and she told me all over again about Arthur and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: their coming marriage. That made me just a little heart-sick, for I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: haven’t heard from Jonathan for a whole month.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _The same day._ I came up here alone, for I am very sad. There was no
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: letter for me. I hope there cannot be anything the matter with Jonathan.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: The clock has just struck nine. I see the lights scattered all over the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: town, sometimes in rows where the streets are, and sometimes singly;
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: they run right up the Esk and die away in the curve of the valley. To my
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: left the view is cut off by a black line of roof of the old house next
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the abbey. The sheep and lambs are bleating in the fields away behind
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: me, and there is a clatter of a donkey’s hoofs up the paved road below.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: The band on the pier is playing a harsh waltz in good time, and further
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: along the quay there is a Salvation Army meeting in a back street.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Neither of the bands hears the other, but up here I hear and see them
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: both. I wonder where Jonathan is and if he is thinking of me! I wish he
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: were here.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _Dr. Seward’s Diary._
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _5 June._--The case of Renfield grows more interesting the more I get to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: understand the man. He has certain qualities very largely developed;
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: selfishness, secrecy, and purpose. I wish I could get at what is the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: object of the latter. He seems to have some settled scheme of his own,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: but what it is I do not yet know. His redeeming quality is a love of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: animals, though, indeed, he has such curious turns in it that I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sometimes imagine he is only abnormally cruel. His pets are of odd
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sorts. Just now his hobby is catching flies. He has at present such a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: quantity that I have had myself to expostulate. To my astonishment, he
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: did not break out into a fury, as I expected, but took the matter in
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: simple seriousness. He thought for a moment, and then said: “May I have
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: three days? I shall clear them away.” Of course, I said that would do. I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: must watch him.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _18 June._--He has turned his mind now to spiders, and has got several
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: very big fellows in a box. He keeps feeding them with his flies, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the number of the latter is becoming sensibly diminished, although he
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: has used half his food in attracting more flies from outside to his
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: room.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _1 July._--His spiders are now becoming as great a nuisance as his
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: flies, and to-day I told him that he must get rid of them. He looked
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: very sad at this, so I said that he must clear out some of them, at all
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: events. He cheerfully acquiesced in this, and I gave him the same time
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: as before for reduction. He disgusted me much while with him, for when a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: horrid blow-fly, bloated with some carrion food, buzzed into the room,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: he caught it, held it exultantly for a few moments between his finger
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and thumb, and, before I knew what he was going to do, put it in his
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: mouth and ate it. I scolded him for it, but he argued quietly that it
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: was very good and very wholesome; that it was life, strong life, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: gave life to him. This gave me an idea, or the rudiment of one. I must
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: watch how he gets rid of his spiders. He has evidently some deep problem
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: in his mind, for he keeps a little note-book in which he is always
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: jotting down something. Whole pages of it are filled with masses of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: figures, generally single numbers added up in batches, and then the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: totals added in batches again, as though he were “focussing” some
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: account, as the auditors put it.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _8 July._--There is a method in his madness, and the rudimentary idea in
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: my mind is growing. It will be a whole idea soon, and then, oh,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: unconscious cerebration! you will have to give the wall to your
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: conscious brother. I kept away from my friend for a few days, so that I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: might notice if there were any change. Things remain as they were except
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: that he has parted with some of his pets and got a new one. He has
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: managed to get a sparrow, and has already partially tamed it. His means
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: of taming is simple, for already the spiders have diminished. Those that
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: do remain, however, are well fed, for he still brings in the flies by
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: tempting them with his food.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _19 July._--We are progressing. My friend has now a whole colony of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sparrows, and his flies and spiders are almost obliterated. When I came
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: in he ran to me and said he wanted to ask me a great favour--a very,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: very great favour; and as he spoke he fawned on me like a dog. I asked
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: him what it was, and he said, with a sort of rapture in his voice and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: bearing:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “A kitten, a nice little, sleek playful kitten, that I can play with,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and teach, and feed--and feed--and feed!” I was not unprepared for this
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: request, for I had noticed how his pets went on increasing in size and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: vivacity, but I did not care that his pretty family of tame sparrows
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: should be wiped out in the same manner as the flies and the spiders; so
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I said I would see about it, and asked him if he would not rather have a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: cat than a kitten. His eagerness betrayed him as he answered:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Oh, yes, I would like a cat! I only asked for a kitten lest you should
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: refuse me a cat. No one would refuse me a kitten, would they?” I shook
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: my head, and said that at present I feared it would not be possible, but
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: that I would see about it. His face fell, and I could see a warning of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: danger in it, for there was a sudden fierce, sidelong look which meant
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: killing. The man is an undeveloped homicidal maniac. I shall test him
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: with his present craving and see how it will work out; then I shall know
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: more.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _10 p. m._--I have visited him again and found him sitting in a corner
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: brooding. When I came in he threw himself on his knees before me and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: implored me to let him have a cat; that his salvation depended upon it.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I was firm, however, and told him that he could not have it, whereupon
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: he went without a word, and sat down, gnawing his fingers, in the corner
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: where I had found him. I shall see him in the morning early.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _20 July._--Visited Renfield very early, before the attendant went his
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: rounds. Found him up and humming a tune. He was spreading out his sugar,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: which he had saved, in the window, and was manifestly beginning his
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: fly-catching again; and beginning it cheerfully and with a good grace. I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: looked around for his birds, and not seeing them, asked him where they
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: were. He replied, without turning round, that they had all flown away.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: There were a few feathers about the room and on his pillow a drop of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: blood. I said nothing, but went and told the keeper to report to me if
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: there were anything odd about him during the day.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _11 a. m._--The attendant has just been to me to say that Renfield has
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: been very sick and has disgorged a whole lot of feathers. “My belief is,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: doctor,” he said, “that he has eaten his birds, and that he just took
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and ate them raw!”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _11 p. m._--I gave Renfield a strong opiate to-night, enough to make
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: even him sleep, and took away his pocket-book to look at it. The thought
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: that has been buzzing about my brain lately is complete, and the theory
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: proved. My homicidal maniac is of a peculiar kind. I shall have to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: invent a new classification for him, and call him a zoöphagous
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: (life-eating) maniac; what he desires is to absorb as many lives as he
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: can, and he has laid himself out to achieve it in a cumulative way. He
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: gave many flies to one spider and many spiders to one bird, and then
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: wanted a cat to eat the many birds. What would have been his later
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: steps? It would almost be worth while to complete the experiment. It
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: might be done if there were only a sufficient cause. Men sneered at
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: vivisection, and yet look at its results to-day! Why not advance science
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: in its most difficult and vital aspect--the knowledge of the brain? Had
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I even the secret of one such mind--did I hold the key to the fancy of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: even one lunatic--I might advance my own branch of science to a pitch
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: compared with which Burdon-Sanderson’s physiology or Ferrier’s
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: brain-knowledge would be as nothing. If only there were a sufficient
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: cause! I must not think too much of this, or I may be tempted; a good
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: cause might turn the scale with me, for may not I too be of an
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: exceptional brain, congenitally?
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: How well the man reasoned; lunatics always do within their own scope. I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: wonder at how many lives he values a man, or if at only one. He has
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: closed the account most accurately, and to-day begun a new record. How
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: many of us begin a new record with each day of our lives?
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: To me it seems only yesterday that my whole life ended with my new hope,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and that truly I began a new record. So it will be until the Great
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Recorder sums me up and closes my ledger account with a balance to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: profit or loss. Oh, Lucy, Lucy, I cannot be angry with you, nor can I be
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: angry with my friend whose happiness is yours; but I must only wait on
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: hopeless and work. Work! work!
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: If I only could have as strong a cause as my poor mad friend there--a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: good, unselfish cause to make me work--that would be indeed happiness.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _Mina Murray’s Journal._
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _26 July._--I am anxious, and it soothes me to express myself here; it
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: is like whispering to one’s self and listening at the same time. And
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: there is also something about the shorthand symbols that makes it
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: different from writing. I am unhappy about Lucy and about Jonathan. I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: had not heard from Jonathan for some time, and was very concerned; but
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: yesterday dear Mr. Hawkins, who is always so kind, sent me a letter from
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: him. I had written asking him if he had heard, and he said the enclosed
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: had just been received. It is only a line dated from Castle Dracula,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and says that he is just starting for home. That is not like Jonathan;
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I do not understand it, and it makes me uneasy. Then, too, Lucy,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: although she is so well, has lately taken to her old habit of walking in
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: her sleep. Her mother has spoken to me about it, and we have decided
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: that I am to lock the door of our room every night. Mrs. Westenra has
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: got an idea that sleep-walkers always go out on roofs of houses and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: along the edges of cliffs and then get suddenly wakened and fall over
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: with a despairing cry that echoes all over the place. Poor dear, she is
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: naturally anxious about Lucy, and she tells me that her husband, Lucy’s
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: father, had the same habit; that he would get up in the night and dress
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: himself and go out, if he were not stopped. Lucy is to be married in the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: autumn, and she is already planning out her dresses and how her house is
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to be arranged. I sympathise with her, for I do the same, only Jonathan
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and I will start in life in a very simple way, and shall have to try to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: make both ends meet. Mr. Holmwood--he is the Hon. Arthur Holmwood, only
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: son of Lord Godalming--is coming up here very shortly--as soon as he can
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: leave town, for his father is not very well, and I think dear Lucy is
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: counting the moments till he comes. She wants to take him up to the seat
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: on the churchyard cliff and show him the beauty of Whitby. I daresay it
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: is the waiting which disturbs her; she will be all right when he
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: arrives.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _27 July._--No news from Jonathan. I am getting quite uneasy about him,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: though why I should I do not know; but I do wish that he would write, if
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: it were only a single line. Lucy walks more than ever, and each night I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: am awakened by her moving about the room. Fortunately, the weather is so
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: hot that she cannot get cold; but still the anxiety and the perpetually
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: being wakened is beginning to tell on me, and I am getting nervous and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: wakeful myself. Thank God, Lucy’s health keeps up. Mr. Holmwood has been
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: suddenly called to Ring to see his father, who has been taken seriously
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: ill. Lucy frets at the postponement of seeing him, but it does not touch
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: her looks; she is a trifle stouter, and her cheeks are a lovely
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: rose-pink. She has lost that anæmic look which she had. I pray it will
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: all last.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _3 August._--Another week gone, and no news from Jonathan, not even to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Mr. Hawkins, from whom I have heard. Oh, I do hope he is not ill. He
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: surely would have written. I look at that last letter of his, but
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: somehow it does not satisfy me. It does not read like him, and yet it is
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: his writing. There is no mistake of that. Lucy has not walked much in
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: her sleep the last week, but there is an odd concentration about her
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: which I do not understand; even in her sleep she seems to be watching
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: me. She tries the door, and finding it locked, goes about the room
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: searching for the key.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _6 August._--Another three days, and no news. This suspense is getting
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: dreadful. If I only knew where to write to or where to go to, I should
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: feel easier; but no one has heard a word of Jonathan since that last
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: letter. I must only pray to God for patience. Lucy is more excitable
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: than ever, but is otherwise well. Last night was very threatening, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the fishermen say that we are in for a storm. I must try to watch it and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: learn the weather signs. To-day is a grey day, and the sun as I write is
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: hidden in thick clouds, high over Kettleness. Everything is grey--except
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the green grass, which seems like emerald amongst it; grey earthy rock;
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: grey clouds, tinged with the sunburst at the far edge, hang over the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: grey sea, into which the sand-points stretch like grey fingers. The sea
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: is tumbling in over the shallows and the sandy flats with a roar,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: muffled in the sea-mists drifting inland. The horizon is lost in a grey
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: mist. All is vastness; the clouds are piled up like giant rocks, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: there is a “brool” over the sea that sounds like some presage of doom.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Dark figures are on the beach here and there, sometimes half shrouded in
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the mist, and seem “men like trees walking.” The fishing-boats are
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: racing for home, and rise and dip in the ground swell as they sweep into
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the harbour, bending to the scuppers. Here comes old Mr. Swales. He is
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: making straight for me, and I can see, by the way he lifts his hat, that
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: he wants to talk....
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I have been quite touched by the change in the poor old man. When he sat
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: down beside me, he said in a very gentle way:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “I want to say something to you, miss.” I could see he was not at ease,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: so I took his poor old wrinkled hand in mine and asked him to speak
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: fully; so he said, leaving his hand in mine:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “I’m afraid, my deary, that I must have shocked you by all the wicked
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: things I’ve been sayin’ about the dead, and such like, for weeks past;
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: but I didn’t mean them, and I want ye to remember that when I’m gone. We
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: aud folks that be daffled, and with one foot abaft the krok-hooal, don’t
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: altogether like to think of it, and we don’t want to feel scart of it;
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: an’ that’s why I’ve took to makin’ light of it, so that I’d cheer up my
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: own heart a bit. But, Lord love ye, miss, I ain’t afraid of dyin’, not a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: bit; only I don’t want to die if I can help it. My time must be nigh at
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: hand now, for I be aud, and a hundred years is too much for any man to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: expect; and I’m so nigh it that the Aud Man is already whettin’ his
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: scythe. Ye see, I can’t get out o’ the habit of caffin’ about it all at
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: once; the chafts will wag as they be used to. Some day soon the Angel of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Death will sound his trumpet for me. But don’t ye dooal an’ greet, my
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: deary!”--for he saw that I was crying--“if he should come this very
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: night I’d not refuse to answer his call. For life be, after all, only a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: waitin’ for somethin’ else than what we’re doin’; and death be all that
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: we can rightly depend on. But I’m content, for it’s comin’ to me, my
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: deary, and comin’ quick. It may be comin’ while we be lookin’ and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: wonderin’. Maybe it’s in that wind out over the sea that’s bringin’ with
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: it loss and wreck, and sore distress, and sad hearts. Look! look!” he
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: cried suddenly. “There’s something in that wind and in the hoast beyont
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: that sounds, and looks, and tastes, and smells like death. It’s in the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: air; I feel it comin’. Lord, make me answer cheerful when my call
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: comes!” He held up his arms devoutly, and raised his hat. His mouth
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: moved as though he were praying. After a few minutes’ silence, he got
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: up, shook hands with me, and blessed me, and said good-bye, and hobbled
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: off. It all touched me, and upset me very much.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I was glad when the coastguard came along, with his spy-glass under his
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: arm. He stopped to talk with me, as he always does, but all the time
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: kept looking at a strange ship.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “I can’t make her out,” he said; “she’s a Russian, by the look of her;
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: but she’s knocking about in the queerest way. She doesn’t know her mind
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: a bit; she seems to see the storm coming, but can’t decide whether to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: run up north in the open, or to put in here. Look there again! She is
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: steered mighty strangely, for she doesn’t mind the hand on the wheel;
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: changes about with every puff of wind. We’ll hear more of her before
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: this time to-morrow.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: CHAPTER VII
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: CUTTING FROM “THE DAILYGRAPH,” 8 AUGUST
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: (_Pasted in Mina Murray’s Journal._)
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: From a Correspondent.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _Whitby_.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: One of the greatest and suddenest storms on record has just been
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: experienced here, with results both strange and unique. The weather had
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: been somewhat sultry, but not to any degree uncommon in the month of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: August. Saturday evening was as fine as was ever known, and the great
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: body of holiday-makers laid out yesterday for visits to Mulgrave Woods,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Robin Hood’s Bay, Rig Mill, Runswick, Staithes, and the various trips in
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the neighbourhood of Whitby. The steamers _Emma_ and _Scarborough_ made
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: trips up and down the coast, and there was an unusual amount of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “tripping” both to and from Whitby. The day was unusually fine till the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: afternoon, when some of the gossips who frequent the East Cliff
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: churchyard, and from that commanding eminence watch the wide sweep of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sea visible to the north and east, called attention to a sudden show of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “mares’-tails” high in the sky to the north-west. The wind was then
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: blowing from the south-west in the mild degree which in barometrical
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: language is ranked “No. 2: light breeze.” The coastguard on duty at once
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: made report, and one old fisherman, who for more than half a century has

author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula

text: storm last night is almost more startling than the thing itself. It
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: small amount of cargo--a number of great wooden boxes filled with mould.

author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
