text: night; was relieved by Abramoff, but did not go to bunk. Men more
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: downcast than ever. All said they expected something of the kind, but
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: would not say more than there was _something_ aboard. Mate getting very
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: impatient with them; feared some trouble ahead.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: On 17 July, yesterday, one of the men, Olgaren, came to my cabin, and in
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: an awestruck way confided to me that he thought there was a strange man
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: aboard the ship. He said that in his watch he had been sheltering
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: behind the deck-house, as there was a rain-storm, when he saw a tall,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: thin man, who was not like any of the crew, come up the companion-way,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and go along the deck forward, and disappear. He followed cautiously,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: but when he got to bows found no one, and the hatchways were all closed.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: He was in a panic of superstitious fear, and I am afraid the panic may
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: spread. To allay it, I shall to-day search entire ship carefully from
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: stem to stern.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Later in the day I got together the whole crew, and told them, as they
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: evidently thought there was some one in the ship, we would search from
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: stem to stern. First mate angry; said it was folly, and to yield to such
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: foolish ideas would demoralise the men; said he would engage to keep
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: them out of trouble with a handspike. I let him take the helm, while the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: rest began thorough search, all keeping abreast, with lanterns: we left
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: no corner unsearched. As there were only the big wooden boxes, there
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: were no odd corners where a man could hide. Men much relieved when
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: search over, and went back to work cheerfully. First mate scowled, but
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: said nothing.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _22 July_.--Rough weather last three days, and all hands busy with
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sails--no time to be frightened. Men seem to have forgotten their dread.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Mate cheerful again, and all on good terms. Praised men for work in bad
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: weather. Passed Gibralter and out through Straits. All well.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _24 July_.--There seems some doom over this ship. Already a hand short,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and entering on the Bay of Biscay with wild weather ahead, and yet last
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: night another man lost--disappeared. Like the first, he came off his
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: watch and was not seen again. Men all in a panic of fear; sent a round
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: robin, asking to have double watch, as they fear to be alone. Mate
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: angry. Fear there will be some trouble, as either he or the men will do
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: some violence.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _28 July_.--Four days in hell, knocking about in a sort of maelstrom,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and the wind a tempest. No sleep for any one. Men all worn out. Hardly
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: know how to set a watch, since no one fit to go on. Second mate
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: volunteered to steer and watch, and let men snatch a few hours’ sleep.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Wind abating; seas still terrific, but feel them less, as ship is
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: steadier.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _29 July_.--Another tragedy. Had single watch to-night, as crew too
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: tired to double. When morning watch came on deck could find no one
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: except steersman. Raised outcry, and all came on deck. Thorough search,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: but no one found. Are now without second mate, and crew in a panic. Mate
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and I agreed to go armed henceforth and wait for any sign of cause.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _30 July_.--Last night. Rejoiced we are nearing England. Weather fine,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: all sails set. Retired worn out; slept soundly; awaked by mate telling
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: me that both man of watch and steersman missing. Only self and mate and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: two hands left to work ship.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _1 August_.--Two days of fog, and not a sail sighted. Had hoped when in
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the English Channel to be able to signal for help or get in somewhere.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Not having power to work sails, have to run before wind. Dare not lower,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: as could not raise them again. We seem to be drifting to some terrible
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: doom. Mate now more demoralised than either of men. His stronger nature
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: seems to have worked inwardly against himself. Men are beyond fear,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: working stolidly and patiently, with minds made up to worst. They are
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Russian, he Roumanian.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _2 August, midnight_.--Woke up from few minutes’ sleep by hearing a cry,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: seemingly outside my port. Could see nothing in fog. Rushed on deck, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: ran against mate. Tells me heard cry and ran, but no sign of man on
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: watch. One more gone. Lord, help us! Mate says we must be past Straits
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: of Dover, as in a moment of fog lifting he saw North Foreland, just as
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: he heard the man cry out. If so we are now off in the North Sea, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: only God can guide us in the fog, which seems to move with us; and God
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: seems to have deserted us.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _3 August_.--At midnight I went to relieve the man at the wheel, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: when I got to it found no one there. The wind was steady, and as we ran
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: before it there was no yawing. I dared not leave it, so shouted for the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: mate. After a few seconds he rushed up on deck in his flannels. He
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: looked wild-eyed and haggard, and I greatly fear his reason has given
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: way. He came close to me and whispered hoarsely, with his mouth to my
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: ear, as though fearing the very air might hear: “_It_ is here; I know
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: it, now. On the watch last night I saw It, like a man, tall and thin,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and ghastly pale. It was in the bows, and looking out. I crept behind
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: It, and gave It my knife; but the knife went through It, empty as the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: air.” And as he spoke he took his knife and drove it savagely into
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: space. Then he went on: “But It is here, and I’ll find It. It is in the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: hold, perhaps in one of those boxes. I’ll unscrew them one by one and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: see. You work the helm.” And, with a warning look and his finger on his
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: lip, he went below. There was springing up a choppy wind, and I could
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: not leave the helm. I saw him come out on deck again with a tool-chest
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and a lantern, and go down the forward hatchway. He is mad, stark,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: raving mad, and it’s no use my trying to stop him. He can’t hurt those
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: big boxes: they are invoiced as “clay,” and to pull them about is as
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: harmless a thing as he can do. So here I stay, and mind the helm, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: write these notes. I can only trust in God and wait till the fog clears.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Then, if I can’t steer to any harbour with the wind that is, I shall cut
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: down sails and lie by, and signal for help....
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: It is nearly all over now. Just as I was beginning to hope that the mate
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: would come out calmer--for I heard him knocking away at something in the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: hold, and work is good for him--there came up the hatchway a sudden,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: startled scream, which made my blood run cold, and up on the deck he
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: came as if shot from a gun--a raging madman, with his eyes rolling and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: his face convulsed with fear. “Save me! save me!” he cried, and then
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: looked round on the blanket of fog. His horror turned to despair, and in
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: a steady voice he said: “You had better come too, captain, before it is
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: too late. _He_ is there. I know the secret now. The sea will save me
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: from Him, and it is all that is left!” Before I could say a word, or
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: move forward to seize him, he sprang on the bulwark and deliberately
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: threw himself into the sea. I suppose I know the secret too, now. It was
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: this madman who had got rid of the men one by one, and now he has
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: followed them himself. God help me! How am I to account for all these
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: horrors when I get to port? _When_ I get to port! Will that ever be?
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _4 August._--Still fog, which the sunrise cannot pierce. I know there is
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sunrise because I am a sailor, why else I know not. I dared not go
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: below, I dared not leave the helm; so here all night I stayed, and in
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the dimness of the night I saw It--Him! God forgive me, but the mate was
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: right to jump overboard. It was better to die like a man; to die like a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sailor in blue water no man can object. But I am captain, and I must not
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: leave my ship. But I shall baffle this fiend or monster, for I shall tie
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: my hands to the wheel when my strength begins to fail, and along with
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: them I shall tie that which He--It!--dare not touch; and then, come good
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: wind or foul, I shall save my soul, and my honour as a captain. I am
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: growing weaker, and the night is coming on. If He can look me in the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: face again, I may not have time to act.... If we are wrecked, mayhap
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: this bottle may be found, and those who find it may understand; if not,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: ... well, then all men shall know that I have been true to my trust. God
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and the Blessed Virgin and the saints help a poor ignorant soul trying
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to do his duty....
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Of course the verdict was an open one. There is no evidence to adduce;
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and whether or not the man himself committed the murders there is now
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: none to say. The folk here hold almost universally that the captain is
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: simply a hero, and he is to be given a public funeral. Already it is
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: arranged that his body is to be taken with a train of boats up the Esk
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: for a piece and then brought back to Tate Hill Pier and up the abbey
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: steps; for he is to be buried in the churchyard on the cliff. The owners
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: of more than a hundred boats have already given in their names as
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: wishing to follow him to the grave.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: No trace has ever been found of the great dog; at which there is much
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: mourning, for, with public opinion in its present state, he would, I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: believe, be adopted by the town. To-morrow will see the funeral; and so
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: will end this one more “mystery of the sea.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _Mina Murray’s Journal._
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _8 August._--Lucy was very restless all night, and I, too, could not
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sleep. The storm was fearful, and as it boomed loudly among the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: chimney-pots, it made me shudder. When a sharp puff came it seemed to be
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: like a distant gun. Strangely enough, Lucy did not wake; but she got up
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: twice and dressed herself. Fortunately, each time I awoke in time and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: managed to undress her without waking her, and got her back to bed. It
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: is a very strange thing, this sleep-walking, for as soon as her will is
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: thwarted in any physical way, her intention, if there be any,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: disappears, and she yields herself almost exactly to the routine of her
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: life.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Early in the morning we both got up and went down to the harbour to see
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: if anything had happened in the night. There were very few people about,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and though the sun was bright, and the air clear and fresh, the big,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: grim-looking waves, that seemed dark themselves because the foam that
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: topped them was like snow, forced themselves in through the narrow mouth
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: of the harbour--like a bullying man going through a crowd. Somehow I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: felt glad that Jonathan was not on the sea last night, but on land. But,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: oh, is he on land or sea? Where is he, and how? I am getting fearfully
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: anxious about him. If I only knew what to do, and could do anything!
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _10 August._--The funeral of the poor sea-captain to-day was most
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: touching. Every boat in the harbour seemed to be there, and the coffin
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: was carried by captains all the way from Tate Hill Pier up to the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: churchyard. Lucy came with me, and we went early to our old seat, whilst
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the cortège of boats went up the river to the Viaduct and came down
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: again. We had a lovely view, and saw the procession nearly all the way.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: The poor fellow was laid to rest quite near our seat so that we stood on
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: it when the time came and saw everything. Poor Lucy seemed much upset.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: She was restless and uneasy all the time, and I cannot but think that
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: her dreaming at night is telling on her. She is quite odd in one thing:
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: she will not admit to me that there is any cause for restlessness; or if
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: there be, she does not understand it herself. There is an additional
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: cause in that poor old Mr. Swales was found dead this morning on our
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: seat, his neck being broken. He had evidently, as the doctor said,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: fallen back in the seat in some sort of fright, for there was a look of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: fear and horror on his face that the men said made them shudder. Poor
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: dear old man! Perhaps he had seen Death with his dying eyes! Lucy is so
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sweet and sensitive that she feels influences more acutely than other
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: people do. Just now she was quite upset by a little thing which I did
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: not much heed, though I am myself very fond of animals. One of the men
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: who came up here often to look for the boats was followed by his dog.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: The dog is always with him. They are both quiet persons, and I never saw
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the man angry, nor heard the dog bark. During the service the dog would
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: not come to its master, who was on the seat with us, but kept a few
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: yards off, barking and howling. Its master spoke to it gently, and then
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: harshly, and then angrily; but it would neither come nor cease to make a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: noise. It was in a sort of fury, with its eyes savage, and all its hairs
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: bristling out like a cat’s tail when puss is on the war-path. Finally
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the man, too, got angry, and jumped down and kicked the dog, and then
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: took it by the scruff of the neck and half dragged and half threw it on
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the tombstone on which the seat is fixed. The moment it touched the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: stone the poor thing became quiet and fell all into a tremble. It did
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: not try to get away, but crouched down, quivering and cowering, and was
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: in such a pitiable state of terror that I tried, though without effect,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to comfort it. Lucy was full of pity, too, but she did not attempt to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: touch the dog, but looked at it in an agonised sort of way. I greatly
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: fear that she is of too super-sensitive a nature to go through the world
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: without trouble. She will be dreaming of this to-night, I am sure. The
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: whole agglomeration of things--the ship steered into port by a dead
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: man; his attitude, tied to the wheel with a crucifix and beads; the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: touching funeral; the dog, now furious and now in terror--will all
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: afford material for her dreams.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I think it will be best for her to go to bed tired out physically, so I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: shall take her for a long walk by the cliffs to Robin Hood’s Bay and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: back. She ought not to have much inclination for sleep-walking then.

text: CHAPTER VIII
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: MINA MURRAY’S JOURNAL
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _Same day, 11 o’clock p. m._--Oh, but I am tired! If it were not that I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: had made my diary a duty I should not open it to-night. We had a lovely
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: walk. Lucy, after a while, was in gay spirits, owing, I think, to some
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: dear cows who came nosing towards us in a field close to the lighthouse,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and frightened the wits out of us. I believe we forgot everything
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: except, of course, personal fear, and it seemed to wipe the slate clean
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and give us a fresh start. We had a capital “severe tea” at Robin Hood’s
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Bay in a sweet little old-fashioned inn, with a bow-window right over
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the seaweed-covered rocks of the strand. I believe we should have
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: shocked the “New Woman” with our appetites. Men are more tolerant, bless
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: them! Then we walked home with some, or rather many, stoppages to rest,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and with our hearts full of a constant dread of wild bulls. Lucy was
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: really tired, and we intended to creep off to bed as soon as we could.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: The young curate came in, however, and Mrs. Westenra asked him to stay
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: for supper. Lucy and I had both a fight for it with the dusty miller; I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: know it was a hard fight on my part, and I am quite heroic. I think that
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: some day the bishops must get together and see about breeding up a new
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: class of curates, who don’t take supper, no matter how they may be
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: pressed to, and who will know when girls are tired. Lucy is asleep and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: breathing softly. She has more colour in her cheeks than usual, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: looks, oh, so sweet. If Mr. Holmwood fell in love with her seeing her
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: only in the drawing-room, I wonder what he would say if he saw her now.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Some of the “New Women” writers will some day start an idea that men and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: women should be allowed to see each other asleep before proposing or
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: accepting. But I suppose the New Woman won’t condescend in future to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: accept; she will do the proposing herself. And a nice job she will make
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: of it, too! There’s some consolation in that. I am so happy to-night,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: because dear Lucy seems better. I really believe she has turned the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: corner, and that we are over her troubles with dreaming. I should be
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: quite happy if I only knew if Jonathan.... God bless and keep him.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _11 August, 3 a. m._--Diary again. No sleep now, so I may as well write.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I am too agitated to sleep. We have had such an adventure, such an
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: agonising experience. I fell asleep as soon as I had closed my diary....
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Suddenly I became broad awake, and sat up, with a horrible sense of fear
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: upon me, and of some feeling of emptiness around me. The room was dark,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: so I could not see Lucy’s bed; I stole across and felt for her. The bed
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: was empty. I lit a match and found that she was not in the room. The
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: door was shut, but not locked, as I had left it. I feared to wake her
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: mother, who has been more than usually ill lately, so threw on some
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: clothes and got ready to look for her. As I was leaving the room it
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: struck me that the clothes she wore might give me some clue to her
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: dreaming intention. Dressing-gown would mean house; dress, outside.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Dressing-gown and dress were both in their places. “Thank God,” I said
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to myself, “she cannot be far, as she is only in her nightdress.” I ran
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: downstairs and looked in the sitting-room. Not there! Then I looked in
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: all the other open rooms of the house, with an ever-growing fear
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: chilling my heart. Finally I came to the hall door and found it open. It
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: was not wide open, but the catch of the lock had not caught. The people
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: of the house are careful to lock the door every night, so I feared that
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Lucy must have gone out as she was. There was no time to think of what
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: might happen; a vague, overmastering fear obscured all details. I took a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: big, heavy shawl and ran out. The clock was striking one as I was in the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Crescent, and there was not a soul in sight. I ran along the North
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Terrace, but could see no sign of the white figure which I expected. At
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the edge of the West Cliff above the pier I looked across the harbour to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the East Cliff, in the hope or fear--I don’t know which--of seeing Lucy
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: in our favourite seat. There was a bright full moon, with heavy black,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: driving clouds, which threw the whole scene into a fleeting diorama of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: light and shade as they sailed across. For a moment or two I could see
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: nothing, as the shadow of a cloud obscured St. Mary’s Church and all
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: around it. Then as the cloud passed I could see the ruins of the abbey
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: coming into view; and as the edge of a narrow band of light as sharp as
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: a sword-cut moved along, the church and the churchyard became gradually
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: visible. Whatever my expectation was, it was not disappointed, for
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: there, on our favourite seat, the silver light of the moon struck a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: half-reclining figure, snowy white. The coming of the cloud was too
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: quick for me to see much, for shadow shut down on light almost
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: immediately; but it seemed to me as though something dark stood behind
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the seat where the white figure shone, and bent over it. What it was,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: whether man or beast, I could not tell; I did not wait to catch another
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: glance, but flew down the steep steps to the pier and along by the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: fish-market to the bridge, which was the only way to reach the East
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Cliff. The town seemed as dead, for not a soul did I see; I rejoiced
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: that it was so, for I wanted no witness of poor Lucy’s condition. The
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: time and distance seemed endless, and my knees trembled and my breath
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: came laboured as I toiled up the endless steps to the abbey. I must have
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: gone fast, and yet it seemed to me as if my feet were weighted with
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: lead, and as though every joint in my body were rusty. When I got almost
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to the top I could see the seat and the white figure, for I was now
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: close enough to distinguish it even through the spells of shadow. There
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: was undoubtedly something, long and black, bending over the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: half-reclining white figure. I called in fright, “Lucy! Lucy!” and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: something raised a head, and from where I was I could see a white face
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and red, gleaming eyes. Lucy did not answer, and I ran on to the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: entrance of the churchyard. As I entered, the church was between me and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the seat, and for a minute or so I lost sight of her. When I came in
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: view again the cloud had passed, and the moonlight struck so brilliantly
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: that I could see Lucy half reclining with her head lying over the back
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: of the seat. She was quite alone, and there was not a sign of any living
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: thing about.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: When I bent over her I could see that she was still asleep. Her lips
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: were parted, and she was breathing--not softly as usual with her, but in
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: long, heavy gasps, as though striving to get her lungs full at every
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: breath. As I came close, she put up her hand in her sleep and pulled the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: collar of her nightdress close around her throat. Whilst she did so
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: there came a little shudder through her, as though she felt the cold. I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: flung the warm shawl over her, and drew the edges tight round her neck,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: for I dreaded lest she should get some deadly chill from the night air,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: unclad as she was. I feared to wake her all at once, so, in order to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: have my hands free that I might help her, I fastened the shawl at her
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: throat with a big safety-pin; but I must have been clumsy in my anxiety
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and pinched or pricked her with it, for by-and-by, when her breathing
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: became quieter, she put her hand to her throat again and moaned. When I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: had her carefully wrapped up I put my shoes on her feet and then began
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: very gently to wake her. At first she did not respond; but gradually she
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: became more and more uneasy in her sleep, moaning and sighing
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: occasionally. At last, as time was passing fast, and, for many other
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: reasons, I wished to get her home at once, I shook her more forcibly,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: till finally she opened her eyes and awoke. She did not seem surprised
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to see me, as, of course, she did not realise all at once where she was.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Lucy always wakes prettily, and even at such a time, when her body must
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: have been chilled with cold, and her mind somewhat appalled at waking
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: unclad in a churchyard at night, she did not lose her grace. She
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: trembled a little, and clung to me; when I told her to come at once with
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: me home she rose without a word, with the obedience of a child. As we
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: passed along, the gravel hurt my feet, and Lucy noticed me wince. She
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: stopped and wanted to insist upon my taking my shoes; but I would not.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: However, when we got to the pathway outside the churchyard, where there
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: was a puddle of water, remaining from the storm, I daubed my feet with
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: mud, using each foot in turn on the other, so that as we went home, no
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: one, in case we should meet any one, should notice my bare feet.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Fortune favoured us, and we got home without meeting a soul. Once we saw
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: a man, who seemed not quite sober, passing along a street in front of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: us; but we hid in a door till he had disappeared up an opening such as
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: there are here, steep little closes, or “wynds,” as they call them in
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Scotland. My heart beat so loud all the time that sometimes I thought I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: should faint. I was filled with anxiety about Lucy, not only for her
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: health, lest she should suffer from the exposure, but for her reputation
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: in case the story should get wind. When we got in, and had washed our
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: feet, and had said a prayer of thankfulness together, I tucked her into
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: bed. Before falling asleep she asked--even implored--me not to say a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: word to any one, even her mother, about her sleep-walking adventure. I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: hesitated at first to promise; but on thinking of the state of her
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: mother’s health, and how the knowledge of such a thing would fret her,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and thinking, too, of how such a story might become distorted--nay,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: infallibly would--in case it should leak out, I thought it wiser to do
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: so. I hope I did right. I have locked the door, and the key is tied to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: my wrist, so perhaps I shall not be again disturbed. Lucy is sleeping
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: soundly; the reflex of the dawn is high and far over the sea....
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _Same day, noon._--All goes well. Lucy slept till I woke her and seemed
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: not to have even changed her side. The adventure of the night does not
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: seem to have harmed her; on the contrary, it has benefited her, for she
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: looks better this morning than she has done for weeks. I was sorry to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: notice that my clumsiness with the safety-pin hurt her. Indeed, it might
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: have been serious, for the skin of her throat was pierced. I must have
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: pinched up a piece of loose skin and have transfixed it, for there are
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: two little red points like pin-pricks, and on the band of her nightdress
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: was a drop of blood. When I apologised and was concerned about it, she
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: laughed and petted me, and said she did not even feel it. Fortunately it
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: cannot leave a scar, as it is so tiny.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _Same day, night._--We passed a happy day. The air was clear, and the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sun bright, and there was a cool breeze. We took our lunch to Mulgrave
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Woods, Mrs. Westenra driving by the road and Lucy and I walking by the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: cliff-path and joining her at the gate. I felt a little sad myself, for
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I could not but feel how _absolutely_ happy it would have been had
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Jonathan been with me. But there! I must only be patient. In the evening
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: we strolled in the Casino Terrace, and heard some good music by Spohr
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and Mackenzie, and went to bed early. Lucy seems more restful than she
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: has been for some time, and fell asleep at once. I shall lock the door
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and secure the key the same as before, though I do not expect any
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: trouble to-night.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _12 August._--My expectations were wrong, for twice during the night I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: was wakened by Lucy trying to get out. She seemed, even in her sleep, to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: be a little impatient at finding the door shut, and went back to bed
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: under a sort of protest. I woke with the dawn, and heard the birds
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: chirping outside of the window. Lucy woke, too, and, I was glad to see,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: was even better than on the previous morning. All her old gaiety of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: manner seemed to have come back, and she came and snuggled in beside me
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and told me all about Arthur. I told her how anxious I was about
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Jonathan, and then she tried to comfort me. Well, she succeeded
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: somewhat, for, though sympathy can’t alter facts, it can help to make
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: them more bearable.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _13 August._--Another quiet day, and to bed with the key on my wrist as
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: before. Again I awoke in the night, and found Lucy sitting up in bed,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: still asleep, pointing to the window. I got up quietly, and pulling
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: aside the blind, looked out. It was brilliant moonlight, and the soft
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: effect of the light over the sea and sky--merged together in one great,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: silent mystery--was beautiful beyond words. Between me and the moonlight
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: flitted a great bat, coming and going in great whirling circles. Once or
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: twice it came quite close, but was, I suppose, frightened at seeing me,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and flitted away across the harbour towards the abbey. When I came back
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: from the window Lucy had lain down again, and was sleeping peacefully.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: She did not stir again all night.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _14 August._--On the East Cliff, reading and writing all day. Lucy seems
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to have become as much in love with the spot as I am, and it is hard to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: get her away from it when it is time to come home for lunch or tea or
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: dinner. This afternoon she made a funny remark. We were coming home for
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: dinner, and had come to the top of the steps up from the West Pier and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: stopped to look at the view, as we generally do. The setting sun, low
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: down in the sky, was just dropping behind Kettleness; the red light was
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: thrown over on the East Cliff and the old abbey, and seemed to bathe
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: everything in a beautiful rosy glow. We were silent for a while, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: suddenly Lucy murmured as if to herself:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “His red eyes again! They are just the same.” It was such an odd
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: expression, coming _apropos_ of nothing, that it quite startled me. I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: slewed round a little, so as to see Lucy well without seeming to stare
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: at her, and saw that she was in a half-dreamy state, with an odd look on
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: her face that I could not quite make out; so I said nothing, but
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: followed her eyes. She appeared to be looking over at our own seat,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: whereon was a dark figure seated alone. I was a little startled myself,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: for it seemed for an instant as if the stranger had great eyes like
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: burning flames; but a second look dispelled the illusion. The red
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sunlight was shining on the windows of St. Mary’s Church behind our
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: seat, and as the sun dipped there was just sufficient change in the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: refraction and reflection to make it appear as if the light moved. I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: called Lucy’s attention to the peculiar effect, and she became herself
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: with a start, but she looked sad all the same; it may have been that she
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: was thinking of that terrible night up there. We never refer to it; so I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: said nothing, and we went home to dinner. Lucy had a headache and went
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: early to bed. I saw her asleep, and went out for a little stroll myself;
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I walked along the cliffs to the westward, and was full of sweet
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sadness, for I was thinking of Jonathan. When coming home--it was then
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: bright moonlight, so bright that, though the front of our part of the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Crescent was in shadow, everything could be well seen--I threw a glance
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: up at our window, and saw Lucy’s head leaning out. I thought that
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: perhaps she was looking out for me, so I opened my handkerchief and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: waved it. She did not notice or make any movement whatever. Just then,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the moonlight crept round an angle of the building, and the light fell
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: on the window. There distinctly was Lucy with her head lying up against
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the side of the window-sill and her eyes shut. She was fast asleep, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: by her, seated on the window-sill, was something that looked like a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: good-sized bird. I was afraid she might get a chill, so I ran upstairs,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: but as I came into the room she was moving back to her bed, fast
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: asleep, and breathing heavily; she was holding her hand to her throat,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: as though to protect it from cold.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I did not wake her, but tucked her up warmly; I have taken care that the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: door is locked and the window securely fastened.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: She looks so sweet as she sleeps; but she is paler than is her wont, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: there is a drawn, haggard look under her eyes which I do not like. I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: fear she is fretting about something. I wish I could find out what it
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: is.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _15 August._--Rose later than usual. Lucy was languid and tired, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: slept on after we had been called. We had a happy surprise at breakfast.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Arthur’s father is better, and wants the marriage to come off soon. Lucy
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: is full of quiet joy, and her mother is glad and sorry at once. Later on
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: in the day she told me the cause. She is grieved to lose Lucy as her
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: very own, but she is rejoiced that she is soon to have some one to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: protect her. Poor dear, sweet lady! She confided to me that she has got
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: her death-warrant. She has not told Lucy, and made me promise secrecy;
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: her doctor told her that within a few months, at most, she must die, for
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: her heart is weakening. At any time, even now, a sudden shock would be
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: almost sure to kill her. Ah, we were wise to keep from her the affair of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the dreadful night of Lucy’s sleep-walking.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _17 August._--No diary for two whole days. I have not had the heart to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: write. Some sort of shadowy pall seems to be coming over our happiness.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: No news from Jonathan, and Lucy seems to be growing weaker, whilst her
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: mother’s hours are numbering to a close. I do not understand Lucy’s
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: fading away as she is doing. She eats well and sleeps well, and enjoys

text: “My dear Jack,--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “I want you to do me a favour. Lucy is ill; that is, she has no special
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: disease, but she looks awful, and is getting worse every day. I have
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: asked her if there is any cause; I do not dare to ask her mother, for to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: disturb the poor lady’s mind about her daughter in her present state of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: health would be fatal. Mrs. Westenra has confided to me that her doom is
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: spoken--disease of the heart--though poor Lucy does not know it yet. I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: am sure that there is something preying on my dear girl’s mind. I am
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: almost distracted when I think of her; to look at her gives me a pang. I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: told her I should ask you to see her, and though she demurred at
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: first--I know why, old fellow--she finally consented. It will be a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: painful task for you, I know, old friend, but it is for _her_ sake, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I must not hesitate to ask, or you to act. You are to come to lunch at
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Hillingham to-morrow, two o’clock, so as not to arouse any suspicion in
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Mrs. Westenra, and after lunch Lucy will take an opportunity of being
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: alone with you. I shall come in for tea, and we can go away together; I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: am filled with anxiety, and want to consult with you alone as soon as I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: can after you have seen her. Do not fail!
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “ARTHUR.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _Telegram, Arthur Holmwood to Seward._
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “_1 September._
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Am summoned to see my father, who is worse. Am writing. Write me fully
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: by to-night’s post to Ring. Wire me if necessary.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _Letter from Dr. Seward to Arthur Holmwood._
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “_2 September._
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “My dear old fellow,--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “With regard to Miss Westenra’s health I hasten to let you know at once
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: that in my opinion there is not any functional disturbance or any malady
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: that I know of. At the same time, I am not by any means satisfied with
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: her appearance; she is woefully different from what she was when I saw
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: her last. Of course you must bear in mind that I did not have full
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: opportunity of examination such as I should wish; our very friendship
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: makes a little difficulty which not even medical science or custom can
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: bridge over. I had better tell you exactly what happened, leaving you to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: draw, in a measure, your own conclusions. I shall then say what I have
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: done and propose doing.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “I found Miss Westenra in seemingly gay spirits. Her mother was present,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and in a few seconds I made up my mind that she was trying all she knew
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to mislead her mother and prevent her from being anxious. I have no
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: doubt she guesses, if she does not know, what need of caution there is.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: We lunched alone, and as we all exerted ourselves to be cheerful, we
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: got, as some kind of reward for our labours, some real cheerfulness
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: amongst us. Then Mrs. Westenra went to lie down, and Lucy was left with
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: me. We went into her boudoir, and till we got there her gaiety remained,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: for the servants were coming and going. As soon as the door was closed,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: however, the mask fell from her face, and she sank down into a chair
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: with a great sigh, and hid her eyes with her hand. When I saw that her
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: high spirits had failed, I at once took advantage of her reaction to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: make a diagnosis. She said to me very sweetly:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “‘I cannot tell you how I loathe talking about myself.’ I reminded her
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: that a doctor’s confidence was sacred, but that you were grievously
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: anxious about her. She caught on to my meaning at once, and settled that
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: matter in a word. ‘Tell Arthur everything you choose. I do not care for
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: myself, but all for him!’ So I am quite free.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “I could easily see that she is somewhat bloodless, but I could not see
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the usual anæmic signs, and by a chance I was actually able to test the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: quality of her blood, for in opening a window which was stiff a cord
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: gave way, and she cut her hand slightly with broken glass. It was a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: slight matter in itself, but it gave me an evident chance, and I secured
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: a few drops of the blood and have analysed them. The qualitative
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: analysis gives a quite normal condition, and shows, I should infer, in
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: itself a vigorous state of health. In other physical matters I was quite
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: satisfied that there is no need for anxiety; but as there must be a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: cause somewhere, I have come to the conclusion that it must be something
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: mental. She complains of difficulty in breathing satisfactorily at
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: times, and of heavy, lethargic sleep, with dreams that frighten her, but
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: regarding which she can remember nothing. She says that as a child she
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: used to walk in her sleep, and that when in Whitby the habit came back,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and that once she walked out in the night and went to East Cliff, where
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Miss Murray found her; but she assures me that of late the habit has not
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: returned. I am in doubt, and so have done the best thing I know of; I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: have written to my old friend and master, Professor Van Helsing, of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Amsterdam, who knows as much about obscure diseases as any one in the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: world. I have asked him to come over, and as you told me that all things
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: were to be at your charge, I have mentioned to him who you are and your
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: relations to Miss Westenra. This, my dear fellow, is in obedience to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: your wishes, for I am only too proud and happy to do anything I can for
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: her. Van Helsing would, I know, do anything for me for a personal
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: reason, so, no matter on what ground he comes, we must accept his
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: wishes. He is a seemingly arbitrary man, but this is because he knows
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: what he is talking about better than any one else. He is a philosopher
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and a metaphysician, and one of the most advanced scientists of his day;
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and he has, I believe, an absolutely open mind. This, with an iron
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: nerve, a temper of the ice-brook, an indomitable resolution,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: self-command, and toleration exalted from virtues to blessings, and the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: kindliest and truest heart that beats--these form his equipment for the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: noble work that he is doing for mankind--work both in theory and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: practice, for his views are as wide as his all-embracing sympathy. I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: tell you these facts that you may know why I have such confidence in
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: him. I have asked him to come at once. I shall see Miss Westenra
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to-morrow again. She is to meet me at the Stores, so that I may not
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: alarm her mother by too early a repetition of my call.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Yours always,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “JOHN SEWARD.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _Letter, Abraham Van Helsing, M. D., D. Ph., D. Lit., etc., etc., to Dr.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Seward._
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “_2 September._
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “My good Friend,--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “When I have received your letter I am already coming to you. By good
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: fortune I can leave just at once, without wrong to any of those who have
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: trusted me. Were fortune other, then it were bad for those who have
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: trusted, for I come to my friend when he call me to aid those he holds
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: dear. Tell your friend that when that time you suck from my wound so
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: swiftly the poison of the gangrene from that knife that our other
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: friend, too nervous, let slip, you did more for him when he wants my
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: aids and you call for them than all his great fortune could do. But it
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: is pleasure added to do for him, your friend; it is to you that I come.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Have then rooms for me at the Great Eastern Hotel, so that I may be near
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to hand, and please it so arrange that we may see the young lady not too
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: late on to-morrow, for it is likely that I may have to return here that
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: night. But if need be I shall come again in three days, and stay longer
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: if it must. Till then good-bye, my friend John.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “VAN HELSING.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _Letter, Dr. Seward to Hon. Arthur Holmwood._
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “_3 September._
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “My dear Art,--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Van Helsing has come and gone. He came on with me to Hillingham, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: found that, by Lucy’s discretion, her mother was lunching out, so that
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: we were alone with her. Van Helsing made a very careful examination of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the patient. He is to report to me, and I shall advise you, for of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: course I was not present all the time. He is, I fear, much concerned,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: but says he must think. When I told him of our friendship and how you
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: trust to me in the matter, he said: ‘You must tell him all you think.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Tell him what I think, if you can guess it, if you will. Nay, I am not
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: jesting. This is no jest, but life and death, perhaps more.’ I asked
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: what he meant by that, for he was very serious. This was when we had
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: come back to town, and he was having a cup of tea before starting on his
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: return to Amsterdam. He would not give me any further clue. You must not
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: be angry with me, Art, because his very reticence means that all his
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: brains are working for her good. He will speak plainly enough when the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: time comes, be sure. So I told him I would simply write an account of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: our visit, just as if I were doing a descriptive special article for
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _The Daily Telegraph_. He seemed not to notice, but remarked that the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: smuts in London were not quite so bad as they used to be when he was a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: student here. I am to get his report to-morrow if he can possibly make
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: it. In any case I am to have a letter.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Well, as to the visit. Lucy was more cheerful than on the day I first
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: saw her, and certainly looked better. She had lost something of the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: ghastly look that so upset you, and her breathing was normal. She was
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: very sweet to the professor (as she always is), and tried to make him
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: feel at ease; though I could see that the poor girl was making a hard
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: struggle for it. I believe Van Helsing saw it, too, for I saw the quick
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: look under his bushy brows that I knew of old. Then he began to chat of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: all things except ourselves and diseases and with such an infinite
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: geniality that I could see poor Lucy’s pretense of animation merge into
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: reality. Then, without any seeming change, he brought the conversation
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: gently round to his visit, and suavely said:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “‘My dear young miss, I have the so great pleasure because you are so
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: much beloved. That is much, my dear, ever were there that which I do not
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: see. They told me you were down in the spirit, and that you were of a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: ghastly pale. To them I say: “Pouf!”’ And he snapped his fingers at me
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and went on: ‘But you and I shall show them how wrong they are. How can
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: he’--and he pointed at me with the same look and gesture as that with
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: which once he pointed me out to his class, on, or rather after, a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: particular occasion which he never fails to remind me of--‘know anything
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: of a young ladies? He has his madmans to play with, and to bring them
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: back to happiness, and to those that love them. It is much to do, and,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: oh, but there are rewards, in that we can bestow such happiness. But the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: young ladies! He has no wife nor daughter, and the young do not tell
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: themselves to the young, but to the old, like me, who have known so many
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sorrows and the causes of them. So, my dear, we will send him away to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: smoke the cigarette in the garden, whiles you and I have little talk all
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to ourselves.’ I took the hint, and strolled about, and presently the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: professor came to the window and called me in. He looked grave, but
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: said: ‘I have made careful examination, but there is no functional
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: cause. With you I agree that there has been much blood lost; it has
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: been, but is not. But the conditions of her are in no way anæmic. I have
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: asked her to send me her maid, that I may ask just one or two question,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: that so I may not chance to miss nothing. I know well what she will say.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: And yet there is cause; there is always cause for everything. I must go
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: back home and think. You must send to me the telegram every day; and if
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: there be cause I shall come again. The disease--for not to be all well
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: is a disease--interest me, and the sweet young dear, she interest me
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: too. She charm me, and for her, if not for you or disease, I come.’
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “As I tell you, he would not say a word more, even when we were alone.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: And so now, Art, you know all I know. I shall keep stern watch. I trust
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: your poor father is rallying. It must be a terrible thing to you, my
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: dear old fellow, to be placed in such a position between two people who
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: are both so dear to you. I know your idea of duty to your father, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: you are right to stick to it; but, if need be, I shall send you word to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: come at once to Lucy; so do not be over-anxious unless you hear from
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: me.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _Dr. Seward’s Diary._
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _4 September._--Zoöphagous patient still keeps up our interest in him.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: He had only one outburst and that was yesterday at an unusual time. Just
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: before the stroke of noon he began to grow restless. The attendant knew
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the symptoms, and at once summoned aid. Fortunately the men came at a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: run, and were just in time, for at the stroke of noon he became so
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: violent that it took all their strength to hold him. In about five
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: minutes, however, he began to get more and more quiet, and finally sank
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: into a sort of melancholy, in which state he has remained up to now. The
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: attendant tells me that his screams whilst in the paroxysm were really
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: appalling; I found my hands full when I got in, attending to some of the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: other patients who were frightened by him. Indeed, I can quite
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: understand the effect, for the sounds disturbed even me, though I was
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: some distance away. It is now after the dinner-hour of the asylum, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: as yet my patient sits in a corner brooding, with a dull, sullen,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: woe-begone look in his face, which seems rather to indicate than to show
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: something directly. I cannot quite understand it.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _Later._--Another change in my patient. At five o’clock I looked in on
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: him, and found him seemingly as happy and contented as he used to be. He
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: was catching flies and eating them, and was keeping note of his capture
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: by making nail-marks on the edge of the door between the ridges of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: padding. When he saw me, he came over and apologised for his bad
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: conduct, and asked me in a very humble, cringing way to be led back to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: his own room and to have his note-book again. I thought it well to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: humour him: so he is back in his room with the window open. He has the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sugar of his tea spread out on the window-sill, and is reaping quite a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: harvest of flies. He is not now eating them, but putting them into a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: box, as of old, and is already examining the corners of his room to find
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: a spider. I tried to get him to talk about the past few days, for any
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: clue to his thoughts would be of immense help to me; but he would not
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: rise. For a moment or two he looked very sad, and said in a sort of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: far-away voice, as though saying it rather to himself than to me:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “All over! all over! He has deserted me. No hope for me now unless I do
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: it for myself!” Then suddenly turning to me in a resolute way, he said:
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Doctor, won’t you be very good to me and let me have a little more
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sugar? I think it would be good for me.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “And the flies?” I said.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Yes! The flies like it, too, and I like the flies; therefore I like
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: it.” And there are people who know so little as to think that madmen do
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: not argue. I procured him a double supply, and left him as happy a man
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: as, I suppose, any in the world. I wish I could fathom his mind.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _Midnight._--Another change in him. I had been to see Miss Westenra,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: whom I found much better, and had just returned, and was standing at our
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: own gate looking at the sunset, when once more I heard him yelling. As
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: his room is on this side of the house, I could hear it better than in
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the morning. It was a shock to me to turn from the wonderful smoky
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
