title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to go to the dining-room and have each a glass of wine. The door flew
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: open for an instant and closed again. The maids shrieked, and then went
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: in a body to the dining-room; and I laid what flowers I had on my dear
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: mother’s breast. When they were there I remembered what Dr. Van Helsing
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: had told me, but I didn’t like to remove them, and, besides, I would
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: have some of the servants to sit up with me now. I was surprised that
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the maids did not come back. I called them, but got no answer, so I went
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to the dining-room to look for them.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: My heart sank when I saw what had happened. They all four lay helpless
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: on the floor, breathing heavily. The decanter of sherry was on the table
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: half full, but there was a queer, acrid smell about. I was suspicious,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and examined the decanter. It smelt of laudanum, and looking on the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sideboard, I found that the bottle which mother’s doctor uses for
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: her--oh! did use--was empty. What am I to do? what am I to do? I am back
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: in the room with mother. I cannot leave her, and I am alone, save for
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the sleeping servants, whom some one has drugged. Alone with the dead! I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: dare not go out, for I can hear the low howl of the wolf through the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: broken window.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: The air seems full of specks, floating and circling in the draught from
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the window, and the lights burn blue and dim. What am I to do? God
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: shield me from harm this night! I shall hide this paper in my breast,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: where they shall find it when they come to lay me out. My dear mother
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: gone! It is time that I go too. Good-bye, dear Arthur, if I should not
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: survive this night. God keep you, dear, and God help me!
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: CHAPTER XII
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: DR. SEWARD’S DIARY
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _18 September._--I drove at once to Hillingham and arrived early.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Keeping my cab at the gate, I went up the avenue alone. I knocked gently
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and rang as quietly as possible, for I feared to disturb Lucy or her
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: mother, and hoped to only bring a servant to the door. After a while,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: finding no response, I knocked and rang again; still no answer. I cursed
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the laziness of the servants that they should lie abed at such an
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: hour--for it was now ten o’clock--and so rang and knocked again, but
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: more impatiently, but still without response. Hitherto I had blamed only
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the servants, but now a terrible fear began to assail me. Was this
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: desolation but another link in the chain of doom which seemed drawing
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: tight around us? Was it indeed a house of death to which I had come, too
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: late? I knew that minutes, even seconds of delay, might mean hours of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: danger to Lucy, if she had had again one of those frightful relapses;
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and I went round the house to try if I could find by chance an entry
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: anywhere.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I could find no means of ingress. Every window and door was fastened and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: locked, and I returned baffled to the porch. As I did so, I heard the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: rapid pit-pat of a swiftly driven horse’s feet. They stopped at the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: gate, and a few seconds later I met Van Helsing running up the avenue.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: When he saw me, he gasped out:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Then it was you, and just arrived. How is she? Are we too late? Did you
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: not get my telegram?”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I answered as quickly and coherently as I could that I had only got his
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: telegram early in the morning, and had not lost a minute in coming here,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and that I could not make any one in the house hear me. He paused and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: raised his hat as he said solemnly:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Then I fear we are too late. God’s will be done!” With his usual
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: recuperative energy, he went on: “Come. If there be no way open to get
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: in, we must make one. Time is all in all to us now.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: We went round to the back of the house, where there was a kitchen
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: window. The Professor took a small surgical saw from his case, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: handing it to me, pointed to the iron bars which guarded the window. I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: attacked them at once and had very soon cut through three of them. Then
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: with a long, thin knife we pushed back the fastening of the sashes and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: opened the window. I helped the Professor in, and followed him. There
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: was no one in the kitchen or in the servants’ rooms, which were close at
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: hand. We tried all the rooms as we went along, and in the dining-room,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: dimly lit by rays of light through the shutters, found four
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: servant-women lying on the floor. There was no need to think them dead,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: for their stertorous breathing and the acrid smell of laudanum in the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: room left no doubt as to their condition. Van Helsing and I looked at
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: each other, and as we moved away he said: “We can attend to them later.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Then we ascended to Lucy’s room. For an instant or two we paused at the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: door to listen, but there was no sound that we could hear. With white
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: faces and trembling hands, we opened the door gently, and entered the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: room.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: How shall I describe what we saw? On the bed lay two women, Lucy and her
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: mother. The latter lay farthest in, and she was covered with a white
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sheet, the edge of which had been blown back by the draught through the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: broken window, showing the drawn, white face, with a look of terror
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: fixed upon it. By her side lay Lucy, with face white and still more
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: drawn. The flowers which had been round her neck we found upon her
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: mother’s bosom, and her throat was bare, showing the two little wounds
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: which we had noticed before, but looking horribly white and mangled.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Without a word the Professor bent over the bed, his head almost touching
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: poor Lucy’s breast; then he gave a quick turn of his head, as of one who
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: listens, and leaping to his feet, he cried out to me:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “It is not yet too late! Quick! quick! Bring the brandy!”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I flew downstairs and returned with it, taking care to smell and taste
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: it, lest it, too, were drugged like the decanter of sherry which I found
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: on the table. The maids were still breathing, but more restlessly, and I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: fancied that the narcotic was wearing off. I did not stay to make sure,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: but returned to Van Helsing. He rubbed the brandy, as on another
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: occasion, on her lips and gums and on her wrists and the palms of her
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: hands. He said to me:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “I can do this, all that can be at the present. You go wake those maids.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Flick them in the face with a wet towel, and flick them hard. Make them
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: get heat and fire and a warm bath. This poor soul is nearly as cold as
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: that beside her. She will need be heated before we can do anything
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: more.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I went at once, and found little difficulty in waking three of the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: women. The fourth was only a young girl, and the drug had evidently
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: affected her more strongly, so I lifted her on the sofa and let her
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sleep. The others were dazed at first, but as remembrance came back to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: them they cried and sobbed in a hysterical manner. I was stern with
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: them, however, and would not let them talk. I told them that one life
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: was bad enough to lose, and that if they delayed they would sacrifice
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Miss Lucy. So, sobbing and crying, they went about their way, half clad
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: as they were, and prepared fire and water. Fortunately, the kitchen and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: boiler fires were still alive, and there was no lack of hot water. We
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: got a bath and carried Lucy out as she was and placed her in it. Whilst
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: we were busy chafing her limbs there was a knock at the hall door. One
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: of the maids ran off, hurried on some more clothes, and opened it. Then
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: she returned and whispered to us that there was a gentleman who had come
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: with a message from Mr. Holmwood. I bade her simply tell him that he
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: must wait, for we could see no one now. She went away with the message,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and, engrossed with our work, I clean forgot all about him.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I never saw in all my experience the Professor work in such deadly
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: earnest. I knew--as he knew--that it was a stand-up fight with death,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and in a pause told him so. He answered me in a way that I did not
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: understand, but with the sternest look that his face could wear:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “If that were all, I would stop here where we are now, and let her fade
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: away into peace, for I see no light in life over her horizon.” He went
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: on with his work with, if possible, renewed and more frenzied vigour.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Presently we both began to be conscious that the heat was beginning to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: be of some effect. Lucy’s heart beat a trifle more audibly to the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: stethoscope, and her lungs had a perceptible movement. Van Helsing’s
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: face almost beamed, and as we lifted her from the bath and rolled her in
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: a hot sheet to dry her he said to me:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “The first gain is ours! Check to the King!”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: We took Lucy into another room, which had by now been prepared, and laid
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: her in bed and forced a few drops of brandy down her throat. I noticed
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: that Van Helsing tied a soft silk handkerchief round her throat. She was
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: still unconscious, and was quite as bad as, if not worse than, we had
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: ever seen her.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Van Helsing called in one of the women, and told her to stay with her
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and not to take her eyes off her till we returned, and then beckoned me
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: out of the room.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “We must consult as to what is to be done,” he said as we descended the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: stairs. In the hall he opened the dining-room door, and we passed in, he
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: closing the door carefully behind him. The shutters had been opened, but
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the blinds were already down, with that obedience to the etiquette of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: death which the British woman of the lower classes always rigidly
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: observes. The room was, therefore, dimly dark. It was, however, light
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: enough for our purposes. Van Helsing’s sternness was somewhat relieved
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: by a look of perplexity. He was evidently torturing his mind about
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: something, so I waited for an instant, and he spoke:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “What are we to do now? Where are we to turn for help? We must have
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: another transfusion of blood, and that soon, or that poor girl’s life
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: won’t be worth an hour’s purchase. You are exhausted already; I am
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: exhausted too. I fear to trust those women, even if they would have
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: courage to submit. What are we to do for some one who will open his
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: veins for her?”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “What’s the matter with me, anyhow?”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: The voice came from the sofa across the room, and its tones brought
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: relief and joy to my heart, for they were those of Quincey Morris. Van
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Helsing started angrily at the first sound, but his face softened and a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: glad look came into his eyes as I cried out: “Quincey Morris!” and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: rushed towards him with outstretched hands.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “What brought you here?” I cried as our hands met.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “I guess Art is the cause.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: He handed me a telegram:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Have not heard from Seward for three days, and am terribly anxious.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Cannot leave. Father still in same condition. Send me word how Lucy is.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Do not delay.--HOLMWOOD.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “I think I came just in the nick of time. You know you have only to tell
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: me what to do.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Van Helsing strode forward, and took his hand, looking him straight in
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the eyes as he said:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “A brave man’s blood is the best thing on this earth when a woman is in
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: trouble. You’re a man and no mistake. Well, the devil may work against
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: us for all he’s worth, but God sends us men when we want them.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Once again we went through that ghastly operation. I have not the heart
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to go through with the details. Lucy had got a terrible shock and it
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: told on her more than before, for though plenty of blood went into her
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: veins, her body did not respond to the treatment as well as on the other
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: occasions. Her struggle back into life was something frightful to see
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and hear. However, the action of both heart and lungs improved, and Van
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Helsing made a subcutaneous injection of morphia, as before, and with
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: good effect. Her faint became a profound slumber. The Professor watched
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: whilst I went downstairs with Quincey Morris, and sent one of the maids
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to pay off one of the cabmen who were waiting. I left Quincey lying down
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: after having a glass of wine, and told the cook to get ready a good
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: breakfast. Then a thought struck me, and I went back to the room where
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Lucy now was. When I came softly in, I found Van Helsing with a sheet or
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: two of note-paper in his hand. He had evidently read it, and was
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: thinking it over as he sat with his hand to his brow. There was a look
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: of grim satisfaction in his face, as of one who has had a doubt solved.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: He handed me the paper saying only: “It dropped from Lucy’s breast when
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: we carried her to the bath.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: When I had read it, I stood looking at the Professor, and after a pause
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: asked him: “In God’s name, what does it all mean? Was she, or is she,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: mad; or what sort of horrible danger is it?” I was so bewildered that I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: did not know what to say more. Van Helsing put out his hand and took the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: paper, saying:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Do not trouble about it now. Forget it for the present. You shall know
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and understand it all in good time; but it will be later. And now what
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: is it that you came to me to say?” This brought me back to fact, and I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: was all myself again.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “I came to speak about the certificate of death. If we do not act
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: properly and wisely, there may be an inquest, and that paper would have
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to be produced. I am in hopes that we need have no inquest, for if we
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: had it would surely kill poor Lucy, if nothing else did. I know, and you
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: know, and the other doctor who attended her knows, that Mrs. Westenra
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: had disease of the heart, and we can certify that she died of it. Let us
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: fill up the certificate at once, and I shall take it myself to the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: registrar and go on to the undertaker.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Good, oh my friend John! Well thought of! Truly Miss Lucy, if she be
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sad in the foes that beset her, is at least happy in the friends that
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: love her. One, two, three, all open their veins for her, besides one old
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: man. Ah yes, I know, friend John; I am not blind! I love you all the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: more for it! Now go.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: In the hall I met Quincey Morris, with a telegram for Arthur telling him
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: that Mrs. Westenra was dead; that Lucy also had been ill, but was now
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: going on better; and that Van Helsing and I were with her. I told him
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: where I was going, and he hurried me out, but as I was going said:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “When you come back, Jack, may I have two words with you all to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: ourselves?” I nodded in reply and went out. I found no difficulty about
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the registration, and arranged with the local undertaker to come up in
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the evening to measure for the coffin and to make arrangements.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: When I got back Quincey was waiting for me. I told him I would see him
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: as soon as I knew about Lucy, and went up to her room. She was still
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sleeping, and the Professor seemingly had not moved from his seat at her
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: side. From his putting his finger to his lips, I gathered that he
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: expected her to wake before long and was afraid of forestalling nature.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: So I went down to Quincey and took him into the breakfast-room, where
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the blinds were not drawn down, and which was a little more cheerful, or
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: rather less cheerless, than the other rooms. When we were alone, he said
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to me:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Jack Seward, I don’t want to shove myself in anywhere where I’ve no
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: right to be; but this is no ordinary case. You know I loved that girl
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and wanted to marry her; but, although that’s all past and gone, I can’t
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: help feeling anxious about her all the same. What is it that’s wrong
title: Dracula

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula

text: _29 September._--I was so absorbed in that wonderful diary of Jonathan
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Harker and that other of his wife that I let the time run on without
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: thinking. Mrs. Harker was not down when the maid came to announce

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I went on with my work. I had just finished Mrs. Harker’s diary, when
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: she came in. She looked sweetly pretty, but very sad, and her eyes were
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: It was like a soul crying out to Almighty God. No one must hear them
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: spoken ever again! See, I have tried to be useful. I have copied out the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I did.”

title: Dracula

title: Dracula

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the knowledge and all the help which we can get. I think that the

title: Dracula

author: Stoker, Bram

text: around. The gaslight which I had left lit for Jonathan, but turned down,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: came only like a tiny red spark through the fog, which had evidently
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: grown thicker and poured into the room. Then it occurred to me that I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: had shut the window before I had come to bed. I would have got out to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: make certain on the point, but some leaden lethargy seemed to chain my
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: limbs and even my will. I lay still and endured; that was all. I closed
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: my eyes, but could still see through my eyelids. (It is wonderful what
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: tricks our dreams play us, and how conveniently we can imagine.) The
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: mist grew thicker and thicker and I could see now how it came in, for I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: could see it like smoke--or with the white energy of boiling
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: water--pouring in, not through the window, but through the joinings of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the door. It got thicker and thicker, till it seemed as if it became
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: concentrated into a sort of pillar of cloud in the room, through the top
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: of which I could see the light of the gas shining like a red eye. Things
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: began to whirl through my brain just as the cloudy column was now
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: whirling in the room, and through it all came the scriptural words “a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: pillar of cloud by day and of fire by night.” Was it indeed some such
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: spiritual guidance that was coming to me in my sleep? But the pillar was
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: composed of both the day and the night-guiding, for the fire was in the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: red eye, which at the thought got a new fascination for me; till, as I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: looked, the fire divided, and seemed to shine on me through the fog like
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: two red eyes, such as Lucy told me of in her momentary mental wandering
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: when, on the cliff, the dying sunlight struck the windows of St. Mary’s
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Church. Suddenly the horror burst upon me that it was thus that Jonathan
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: had seen those awful women growing into reality through the whirling mist
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: in the moonlight, and in my dream I must have fainted, for all became
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: black darkness. The last conscious effort which imagination made was to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: show me a livid white face bending over me out of the mist. I must be
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: careful of such dreams, for they would unseat one’s reason if there were
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: too much of them. I would get Dr. Van Helsing or Dr. Seward to prescribe
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: something for me which would make me sleep, only that I fear to alarm
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: them. Such a dream at the present time would become woven into their
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: fears for me. To-night I shall strive hard to sleep naturally. If I do
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: not, I shall to-morrow night get them to give me a dose of chloral; that
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: cannot hurt me for once, and it will give me a good night’s sleep. Last
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: night tired me more than if I had not slept at all.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _2 October 10 p. m._--Last night I slept, but did not dream. I must have
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: slept soundly, for I was not waked by Jonathan coming to bed; but the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sleep has not refreshed me, for to-day I feel terribly weak and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: spiritless. I spent all yesterday trying to read, or lying down dozing.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: In the afternoon Mr. Renfield asked if he might see me. Poor man, he was
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: very gentle, and when I came away he kissed my hand and bade God bless
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: me. Some way it affected me much; I am crying when I think of him. This
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: is a new weakness, of which I must be careful. Jonathan would be
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: miserable if he knew I had been crying. He and the others were out till
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: dinner-time, and they all came in tired. I did what I could to brighten
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: them up, and I suppose that the effort did me good, for I forgot how
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: tired I was. After dinner they sent me to bed, and all went off to smoke
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: together, as they said, but I knew that they wanted to tell each other
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: of what had occurred to each during the day; I could see from Jonathan’s
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: manner that he had something important to communicate. I was not so
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sleepy as I should have been; so before they went I asked Dr. Seward to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: give me a little opiate of some kind, as I had not slept well the night
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: before. He very kindly made me up a sleeping draught, which he gave to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: me, telling me that it would do me no harm, as it was very mild.... I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: have taken it, and am waiting for sleep, which still keeps aloof. I hope
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I have not done wrong, for as sleep begins to flirt with me, a new fear
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: comes: that I may have been foolish in thus depriving myself of the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: power of waking. I might want it. Here comes sleep. Good-night.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: CHAPTER XX
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: JONATHAN HARKER’S JOURNAL
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _1 October, evening._--I found Thomas Snelling in his house at Bethnal
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Green, but unhappily he was not in a condition to remember anything. The
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: very prospect of beer which my expected coming had opened to him had
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: proved too much, and he had begun too early on his expected debauch. I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: learned, however, from his wife, who seemed a decent, poor soul, that he
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: was only the assistant to Smollet, who of the two mates was the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: responsible person. So off I drove to Walworth, and found Mr. Joseph
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Smollet at home and in his shirtsleeves, taking a late tea out of a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: saucer. He is a decent, intelligent fellow, distinctly a good, reliable
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: type of workman, and with a headpiece of his own. He remembered all
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: about the incident of the boxes, and from a wonderful dog’s-eared
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: notebook, which he produced from some mysterious receptacle about the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: seat of his trousers, and which had hieroglyphical entries in thick,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: half-obliterated pencil, he gave me the destinations of the boxes. There
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: were, he said, six in the cartload which he took from Carfax and left at
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: 197, Chicksand Street, Mile End New Town, and another six which he
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: deposited at Jamaica Lane, Bermondsey. If then the Count meant to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: scatter these ghastly refuges of his over London, these places were
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: chosen as the first of delivery, so that later he might distribute more
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: fully. The systematic manner in which this was done made me think that
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: he could not mean to confine himself to two sides of London. He was now
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: fixed on the far east of the northern shore, on the east of the southern
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: shore, and on the south. The north and west were surely never meant to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: be left out of his diabolical scheme--let alone the City itself and the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: very heart of fashionable London in the south-west and west. I went back
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to Smollet, and asked him if he could tell us if any other boxes had
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: been taken from Carfax.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: He replied:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Well, guv’nor, you’ve treated me wery ’an’some”--I had given him half a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sovereign--“an’ I’ll tell yer all I know. I heard a man by the name of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Bloxam say four nights ago in the ’Are an’ ’Ounds, in Pincher’s Alley,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: as ’ow he an’ his mate ’ad ’ad a rare dusty job in a old ’ouse at
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Purfect. There ain’t a-many such jobs as this ’ere, an’ I’m thinkin’
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: that maybe Sam Bloxam could tell ye summut.” I asked if he could tell me
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: where to find him. I told him that if he could get me the address it
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: would be worth another half-sovereign to him. So he gulped down the rest
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: of his tea and stood up, saying that he was going to begin the search
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: then and there. At the door he stopped, and said:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Look ’ere, guv’nor, there ain’t no sense in me a-keepin’ you ’ere. I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: may find Sam soon, or I mayn’t; but anyhow he ain’t like to be in a way
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to tell ye much to-night. Sam is a rare one when he starts on the booze.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: If you can give me a envelope with a stamp on it, and put yer address on
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: it, I’ll find out where Sam is to be found and post it ye to-night. But
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: ye’d better be up arter ’im soon in the mornin’, or maybe ye won’t ketch
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: ’im; for Sam gets off main early, never mind the booze the night afore.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: This was all practical, so one of the children went off with a penny to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: buy an envelope and a sheet of paper, and to keep the change. When she
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: came back, I addressed the envelope and stamped it, and when Smollet had
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: again faithfully promised to post the address when found, I took my way
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to home. We’re on the track anyhow. I am tired to-night, and want sleep.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Mina is fast asleep, and looks a little too pale; her eyes look as
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: though she had been crying. Poor dear, I’ve no doubt it frets her to be
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: kept in the dark, and it may make her doubly anxious about me and the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: others. But it is best as it is. It is better to be disappointed and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: worried in such a way now than to have her nerve broken. The doctors
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: were quite right to insist on her being kept out of this dreadful
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: business. I must be firm, for on me this particular burden of silence
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: must rest. I shall not ever enter on the subject with her under any
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: circumstances. Indeed, it may not be a hard task, after all, for she
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: herself has become reticent on the subject, and has not spoken of the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Count or his doings ever since we told her of our decision.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _2 October, evening._--A long and trying and exciting day. By the first
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: post I got my directed envelope with a dirty scrap of paper enclosed, on
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: which was written with a carpenter’s pencil in a sprawling hand:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Sam Bloxam, Korkrans, 4, Poters Cort, Bartel Street, Walworth. Arsk for
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the depite.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I got the letter in bed, and rose without waking Mina. She looked heavy
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and sleepy and pale, and far from well. I determined not to wake her,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: but that, when I should return from this new search, I would arrange for
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: her going back to Exeter. I think she would be happier in our own home,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: with her daily tasks to interest her, than in being here amongst us and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: in ignorance. I only saw Dr. Seward for a moment, and told him where I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: was off to, promising to come back and tell the rest so soon as I should
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: have found out anything. I drove to Walworth and found, with some
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: difficulty, Potter’s Court. Mr. Smollet’s spelling misled me, as I asked
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: for Poter’s Court instead of Potter’s Court. However, when I had found
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the court, I had no difficulty in discovering Corcoran’s lodging-house.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: When I asked the man who came to the door for the “depite,” he shook his
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: head, and said: “I dunno ’im. There ain’t no such a person ’ere; I never
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: ’eard of ’im in all my bloomin’ days. Don’t believe there ain’t nobody
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: of that kind livin’ ere or anywheres.” I took out Smollet’s letter, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: as I read it it seemed to me that the lesson of the spelling of the name
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: of the court might guide me. “What are you?” I asked.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “I’m the depity,” he answered. I saw at once that I was on the right
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: track; phonetic spelling had again misled me. A half-crown tip put the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: deputy’s knowledge at my disposal, and I learned that Mr. Bloxam, who
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: had slept off the remains of his beer on the previous night at
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Corcoran’s, had left for his work at Poplar at five o’clock that
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: morning. He could not tell me where the place of work was situated, but
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: he had a vague idea that it was some kind of a “new-fangled ware’us”;
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and with this slender clue I had to start for Poplar. It was twelve
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: o’clock before I got any satisfactory hint of such a building, and this
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I got at a coffee-shop, where some workmen were having their dinner. One
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: of these suggested that there was being erected at Cross Angel Street a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: new “cold storage” building; and as this suited the condition of a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “new-fangled ware’us,” I at once drove to it. An interview with a surly
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: gatekeeper and a surlier foreman, both of whom were appeased with the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: coin of the realm, put me on the track of Bloxam; he was sent for on my
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: suggesting that I was willing to pay his day’s wages to his foreman for
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the privilege of asking him a few questions on a private matter. He was
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: a smart enough fellow, though rough of speech and bearing. When I had
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: promised to pay for his information and given him an earnest, he told me
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: that he had made two journeys between Carfax and a house in Piccadilly,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and had taken from this house to the latter nine great boxes--“main
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: heavy ones”--with a horse and cart hired by him for this purpose. I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: asked him if he could tell me the number of the house in Piccadilly, to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: which he replied:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Well, guv’nor, I forgits the number, but it was only a few doors from a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: big white church or somethink of the kind, not long built. It was a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: dusty old ’ouse, too, though nothin’ to the dustiness of the ’ouse we
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: tooked the bloomin’ boxes from.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “How did you get into the houses if they were both empty?”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “There was the old party what engaged me a-waitin’ in the ’ouse at
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Purfleet. He ’elped me to lift the boxes and put them in the dray. Curse
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: me, but he was the strongest chap I ever struck, an’ him a old feller,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: with a white moustache, one that thin you would think he couldn’t throw
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: a shadder.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: How this phrase thrilled through me!
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Why, ’e took up ’is end o’ the boxes like they was pounds of tea, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: me a-puffin’ an’ a-blowin’ afore I could up-end mine anyhow--an’ I’m no
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: chicken, neither.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “How did you get into the house in Piccadilly?” I asked.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “He was there too. He must ’a’ started off and got there afore me, for
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: when I rung of the bell he kem an’ opened the door ’isself an’ ’elped me
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to carry the boxes into the ’all.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “The whole nine?” I asked.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Yus; there was five in the first load an’ four in the second. It was
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: main dry work, an’ I don’t so well remember ’ow I got ’ome.” I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: interrupted him:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Were the boxes left in the hall?”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Yus; it was a big ’all, an’ there was nothin’ else in it.” I made one
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: more attempt to further matters:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “You didn’t have any key?”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Never used no key nor nothink. The old gent, he opened the door ’isself
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: an’ shut it again when I druv off. I don’t remember the last time--but
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: that was the beer.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “And you can’t remember the number of the house?”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “No, sir. But ye needn’t have no difficulty about that. It’s a ’igh ’un
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: with a stone front with a bow on it, an’ ’igh steps up to the door. I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: know them steps, ’avin’ ’ad to carry the boxes up with three loafers
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: what come round to earn a copper. The old gent give them shillin’s, an’
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: they seein’ they got so much, they wanted more; but ’e took one of them
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: by the shoulder and was like to throw ’im down the steps, till the lot
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: of them went away cussin’.” I thought that with this description I could
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: find the house, so, having paid my friend for his information, I started
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: off for Piccadilly. I had gained a new painful experience; the Count
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: could, it was evident, handle the earth-boxes himself. If so, time was
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: precious; for, now that he had achieved a certain amount of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: distribution, he could, by choosing his own time, complete the task
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: unobserved. At Piccadilly Circus I discharged my cab, and walked
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: westward; beyond the Junior Constitutional I came across the house
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: described, and was satisfied that this was the next of the lairs
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: arranged by Dracula. The house looked as though it had been long
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: untenanted. The windows were encrusted with dust, and the shutters were
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: up. All the framework was black with time, and from the iron the paint
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: had mostly scaled away. It was evident that up to lately there had been
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: a large notice-board in front of the balcony; it had, however, been
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: roughly torn away, the uprights which had supported it still remaining.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Behind the rails of the balcony I saw there were some loose boards,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: whose raw edges looked white. I would have given a good deal to have
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: been able to see the notice-board intact, as it would, perhaps, have

text: _3 October._--The time seemed terribly long whilst we were waiting for
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: the coming of Godalming and Quincey Morris. The Professor tried to keep
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: The poor fellow is overwhelmed in a misery that is appalling to see.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Last night he was a frank, happy-looking man, with strong, youthful
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula

author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula

text: night. I am feeling very sleepy; the cold is perhaps beginning to tell
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: upon me, and nature must have rest some time. Godalming insists that he
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: shall keep the first watch. God bless him for all his goodness to poor
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: dear Mina and me.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _2 November, morning._--It is broad daylight. That good fellow would not
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: wake me. He says it would have been a sin to, for I slept peacefully and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: was forgetting my trouble. It seems brutally selfish to me to have slept
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: so long, and let him watch all night; but he was quite right. I am a new
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: man this morning; and, as I sit here and watch him sleeping, I can do
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: all that is necessary both as to minding the engine, steering, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: keeping watch. I can feel that my strength and energy are coming back to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: me. I wonder where Mina is now, and Van Helsing. They should have got to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Veresti about noon on Wednesday. It would take them some time to get the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: carriage and horses; so if they had started and travelled hard, they
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: would be about now at the Borgo Pass. God guide and help them! I am
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: afraid to think what may happen. If we could only go faster! but we
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: cannot; the engines are throbbing and doing their utmost. I wonder how
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Dr. Seward and Mr. Morris are getting on. There seem to be endless
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: streams running down the mountains into this river, but as none of them
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: are very large--at present, at all events, though they are terrible
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: doubtless in winter and when the snow melts--the horsemen may not have
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: met much obstruction. I hope that before we get to Strasba we may see
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: them; for if by that time we have not overtaken the Count, it may be
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: necessary to take counsel together what to do next.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _Dr. Seward’s Diary._
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _2 November._--Three days on the road. No news, and no time to write it
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: if there had been, for every moment is precious. We have had only the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: rest needful for the horses; but we are both bearing it wonderfully.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Those adventurous days of ours are turning up useful. We must push on;
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: we shall never feel happy till we get the launch in sight again.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _3 November._--We heard at Fundu that the launch had gone up the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Bistritza. I wish it wasn’t so cold. There are signs of snow coming; and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: if it falls heavy it will stop us. In such case we must get a sledge and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: go on, Russian fashion.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _4 November._--To-day we heard of the launch having been detained by an
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: accident when trying to force a way up the rapids. The Slovak boats get
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: up all right, by aid of a rope and steering with knowledge. Some went up
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: only a few hours before. Godalming is an amateur fitter himself, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: evidently it was he who put the launch in trim again. Finally, they got
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: up the rapids all right, with local help, and are off on the chase
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: afresh. I fear that the boat is not any better for the accident; the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: peasantry tell us that after she got upon smooth water again, she kept
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: stopping every now and again so long as she was in sight. We must push
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: on harder than ever; our help may be wanted soon.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _Mina Harker’s Journal._
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _31 October._--Arrived at Veresti at noon. The Professor tells me that
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: this morning at dawn he could hardly hypnotise me at all, and that all I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: could say was: “dark and quiet.” He is off now buying a carriage and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: horses. He says that he will later on try to buy additional horses, so
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: that we may be able to change them on the way. We have something more
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: than 70 miles before us. The country is lovely, and most interesting; if
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: only we were under different conditions, how delightful it would be to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: see it all. If Jonathan and I were driving through it alone what a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: pleasure it would be. To stop and see people, and learn something of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: their life, and to fill our minds and memories with all the colour and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: picturesqueness of the whole wild, beautiful country and the quaint
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: people! But, alas!--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _Later._--Dr. Van Helsing has returned. He has got the carriage and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: horses; we are to have some dinner, and to start in an hour. The
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: landlady is putting us up a huge basket of provisions; it seems enough
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: for a company of soldiers. The Professor encourages her, and whispers to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: me that it may be a week before we can get any good food again. He has
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: been shopping too, and has sent home such a wonderful lot of fur coats
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and wraps, and all sorts of warm things. There will not be any chance of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: our being cold.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: We shall soon be off. I am afraid to think what may happen to us. We are
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: truly in the hands of God. He alone knows what may be, and I pray Him,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: with all the strength of my sad and humble soul, that He will watch over
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: my beloved husband; that whatever may happen, Jonathan may know that I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: loved him and honoured him more than I can say, and that my latest and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: truest thought will be always for him.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: CHAPTER XXVII
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: MINA HARKER’S JOURNAL
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _1 November._--All day long we have travelled, and at a good speed. The
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: horses seem to know that they are being kindly treated, for they go
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: willingly their full stage at best speed. We have now had so many
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: changes and find the same thing so constantly that we are encouraged to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: think that the journey will be an easy one. Dr. Van Helsing is laconic;
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: he tells the farmers that he is hurrying to Bistritz, and pays them well
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to make the exchange of horses. We get hot soup, or coffee, or tea; and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: off we go. It is a lovely country; full of beauties of all imaginable
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: kinds, and the people are brave, and strong, and simple, and seem full
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: of nice qualities. They are _very, very_ superstitious. In the first
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: house where we stopped, when the woman who served us saw the scar on my
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: forehead, she crossed herself and put out two fingers towards me, to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: keep off the evil eye. I believe they went to the trouble of putting an
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: extra amount of garlic into our food; and I can’t abide garlic. Ever
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: since then I have taken care not to take off my hat or veil, and so have
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: escaped their suspicions. We are travelling fast, and as we have no
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: driver with us to carry tales, we go ahead of scandal; but I daresay
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: that fear of the evil eye will follow hard behind us all the way. The
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Professor seems tireless; all day he would not take any rest, though he
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: made me sleep for a long spell. At sunset time he hypnotised me, and he
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: says that I answered as usual “darkness, lapping water and creaking
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: wood”; so our enemy is still on the river. I am afraid to think of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Jonathan, but somehow I have now no fear for him, or for myself. I write
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: this whilst we wait in a farmhouse for the horses to be got ready. Dr.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Van Helsing is sleeping. Poor dear, he looks very tired and old and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: grey, but his mouth is set as firmly as a conqueror’s; even in his sleep
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: he is instinct with resolution. When we have well started I must make
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: him rest whilst I drive. I shall tell him that we have days before us,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and we must not break down when most of all his strength will be
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: needed.... All is ready; we are off shortly.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _2 November, morning._--I was successful, and we took turns driving all
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: night; now the day is on us, bright though cold. There is a strange
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: heaviness in the air--I say heaviness for want of a better word; I mean
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: that it oppresses us both. It is very cold, and only our warm furs keep
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: us comfortable. At dawn Van Helsing hypnotised me; he says I answered
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “darkness, creaking wood and roaring water,” so the river is changing as
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: they ascend. I do hope that my darling will not run any chance of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: danger--more than need be; but we are in God’s hands.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _2 November, night._--All day long driving. The country gets wilder as
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: we go, and the great spurs of the Carpathians, which at Veresti seemed
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: so far from us and so low on the horizon, now seem to gather round us
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and tower in front. We both seem in good spirits; I think we make an
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: effort each to cheer the other; in the doing so we cheer ourselves. Dr.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Van Helsing says that by morning we shall reach the Borgo Pass. The
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: houses are very few here now, and the Professor says that the last horse
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: we got will have to go on with us, as we may not be able to change. He
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: got two in addition to the two we changed, so that now we have a rude
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: four-in-hand. The dear horses are patient and good, and they give us no
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: trouble. We are not worried with other travellers, and so even I can
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: drive. We shall get to the Pass in daylight; we do not want to arrive
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: before. So we take it easy, and have each a long rest in turn. Oh, what
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: will to-morrow bring to us? We go to seek the place where my poor
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: darling suffered so much. God grant that we may be guided aright, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: that He will deign to watch over my husband and those dear to us both,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and who are in such deadly peril. As for me, I am not worthy in His
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sight. Alas! I am unclean to His eyes, and shall be until He may deign
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to let me stand forth in His sight as one of those who have not incurred
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: His wrath.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _Memorandum by Abraham Van Helsing._
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _4 November._--This to my old and true friend John Seward, M.D., of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Purfleet, London, in case I may not see him. It may explain. It is
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: morning, and I write by a fire which all the night I have kept
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: alive--Madam Mina aiding me. It is cold, cold; so cold that the grey
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: heavy sky is full of snow, which when it falls will settle for all
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: winter as the ground is hardening to receive it. It seems to have
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: affected Madam Mina; she has been so heavy of head all day that she was
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: not like herself. She sleeps, and sleeps, and sleeps! She who is usual
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: so alert, have done literally nothing all the day; she even have lost
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: her appetite. She make no entry into her little diary, she who write so
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: faithful at every pause. Something whisper to me that all is not well.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: However, to-night she is more _vif_. Her long sleep all day have refresh
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and restore her, for now she is all sweet and bright as ever. At sunset
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: I try to hypnotise her, but alas! with no effect; the power has grown
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: less and less with each day, and to-night it fail me altogether. Well,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: God’s will be done--whatever it may be, and whithersoever it may lead!
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Now to the historical, for as Madam Mina write not in her stenography, I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: must, in my cumbrous old fashion, that so each day of us may not go
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: unrecorded.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: We got to the Borgo Pass just after sunrise yesterday morning. When I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: saw the signs of the dawn I got ready for the hypnotism. We stopped our
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: carriage, and got down so that there might be no disturbance. I made a
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: couch with furs, and Madam Mina, lying down, yield herself as usual, but
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: more slow and more short time than ever, to the hypnotic sleep. As
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: before, came the answer: “darkness and the swirling of water.” Then she
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: woke, bright and radiant and we go on our way and soon reach the Pass.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: At this time and place, she become all on fire with zeal; some new
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: guiding power be in her manifested, for she point to a road and say:--
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “This is the way.”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “How know you it?” I ask.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: “Of course I know it,” she answer, and with a pause, add: “Have not my
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Jonathan travelled it and wrote of his travel?”
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: At first I think somewhat strange, but soon I see that there be only one
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: such by-road. It is used but little, and very different from the coach
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: road from the Bukovina to Bistritz, which is more wide and hard, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: more of use.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: So we came down this road; when we meet other ways--not always were we
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sure that they were roads at all, for they be neglect and light snow
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: have fallen--the horses know and they only. I give rein to them, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: they go on so patient. By-and-by we find all the things which Jonathan
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: have note in that wonderful diary of him. Then we go on for long, long
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: hours and hours. At the first, I tell Madam Mina to sleep; she try, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: she succeed. She sleep all the time; till at the last, I feel myself to
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: suspicious grow, and attempt to wake her. But she sleep on, and I may
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: not wake her though I try. I do not wish to try too hard lest I harm
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: her; for I know that she have suffer much, and sleep at times be
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: all-in-all to her. I think I drowse myself, for all of sudden I feel
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: guilt, as though I have done something; I find myself bolt up, with the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: reins in my hand, and the good horses go along jog, jog, just as ever. I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: look down and find Madam Mina still sleep. It is now not far off sunset
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: time, and over the snow the light of the sun flow in big yellow flood,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: so that we throw great long shadow on where the mountain rise so steep.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: For we are going up, and up; and all is oh! so wild and rocky, as though
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: it were the end of the world.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Then I arouse Madam Mina. This time she wake with not much trouble, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: then I try to put her to hypnotic sleep. But she sleep not, being as
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: though I were not. Still I try and try, till all at once I find her and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: myself in dark; so I look round, and find that the sun have gone down.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: Madam Mina laugh, and I turn and look at her. She is now quite awake,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and look so well as I never saw her since that night at Carfax when we
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: first enter the Count’s house. I am amaze, and not at ease then; but she
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: is so bright and tender and thoughtful for me that I forget all fear. I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: light a fire, for we have brought supply of wood with us, and she
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: prepare food while I undo the horses and set them, tethered in shelter,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to feed. Then when I return to the fire she have my supper ready. I go
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to help her; but she smile, and tell me that she have eat already--that
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: she was so hungry that she would not wait. I like it not, and I have
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: grave doubts; but I fear to affright her, and so I am silent of it. She
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: help me and I eat alone; and then we wrap in fur and lie beside the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: fire, and I tell her to sleep while I watch. But presently I forget all
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: of watching; and when I sudden remember that I watch, I find her lying
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: quiet, but awake, and looking at me with so bright eyes. Once, twice
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: more the same occur, and I get much sleep till before morning. When I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: wake I try to hypnotise her; but alas! though she shut her eyes
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: obedient, she may not sleep. The sun rise up, and up, and up; and then
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sleep come to her too late, but so heavy that she will not wake. I have
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to lift her up, and place her sleeping in the carriage when I have
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: harnessed the horses and made all ready. Madam still sleep, and she look
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: in her sleep more healthy and more redder than before. And I like it
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: not. And I am afraid, afraid, afraid!--I am afraid of all things--even
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: to think but I must go on my way. The stake we play for is life and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: death, or more than these, and we must not flinch.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: _5 November, morning._--Let me be accurate in everything, for though you
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: and I have seen some strange things together, you may at the first think
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: that I, Van Helsing, am mad--that the many horrors and the so long
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: strain on nerves has at the last turn my brain.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: All yesterday we travel, ever getting closer to the mountains, and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: moving into a more and more wild and desert land. There are great,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: frowning precipices and much falling water, and Nature seem to have held
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: sometime her carnival. Madam Mina still sleep and sleep; and though I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: did have hunger and appeased it, I could not waken her--even for food. I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: began to fear that the fatal spell of the place was upon her, tainted as
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: she is with that Vampire baptism. “Well,” said I to myself, “if it be
title: Dracula

text: _6 November._--It was late in the afternoon when the Professor and I
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: took our way towards the east whence I knew Jonathan was coming. We did
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: not go fast, though the way was steeply downhill, for we had to take
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: heavy rugs and wraps with us; we dared not face the possibility of being

author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: could see through the snowfall, there was not even the sign of
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula

author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

text: on any side. There was something wild and uncanny about the place. We
title: Dracula

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: that he was trying to seek some strategic point, where we would be less
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: could trace it through the drifted snow.
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: In a little while the Professor signalled to me, so I got up and joined
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: him. He had found a wonderful spot, a sort of natural hollow in a rock,

author: Stoker, Bram

text: with an entrance like a doorway between two boulders. He took me by the
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: hand and drew me in: “See!” he said, “here you will be in shelter; and
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: reproach me. Taking his field-glasses from the case, he stood on the top
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram

text: of the rock, and began to search the horizon. Suddenly he called out:--

author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

author: Stoker, Bram

text: voice:--

text: It was strange to see the snow falling in such heavy flakes close to us,
title: Dracula
author: Stoker, Bram
